cards.title: Spellmachinist's Bell Bearing
cards.description: Bell bearing of Spellmachinist Rabbath.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: String-Seller's Bell Bearing
cards.description: The bell bearing of a hornsent string-seller who did not survive the war.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Ymir's Bell Bearing
cards.description: Bell bearing of Count Ymir, Mother of Fingers, found upon his perished flesh.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Ancient Meteoric Ore Greatsword
cards.description:
One of the treasures of the ruined forges.
Greatsword of ancient meteoric ore, ending in a sharp point.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Ansbach's Longbow
cards.description:
Weapon of Sir Ansbach.
A longbow consisting of two bow bodies and two strings.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Anvil Hammer
cards.description:
One of the treasures of the ruined forges.
A tremendous hammer that incorporates as its head the red-hot anvil of the forge.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Backhand Blade
cards.description:
Curved blade wielded with a backhand grip.
A type of weapon wielded by hornsent for generations.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Barbed Staff-Spear
cards.description:
Greatstaff of Jori, elder inquisitor.
The tip bristles with golden barbs symbolic of the inquisition's torture, allowing one to wield the staff as a greatspear.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Beast Claw
cards.description:
Weapon in the form of a carnivorous beast's vicious claws.
Used to perform bestial slashing attacks uncanny to humankind.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Black Steel Greathammer
cards.description:
Weapon of the Black Knights, servants of Messmer the Impaler. Greathammer of black steel with decorative embellishments in gold.
Blessed with an Erdtree incantation. Further holy imbuement will amplify the effect and greatly increase the armament's power. Guard Counters cause a burst of power.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Black Steel Greatshield
cards.description: Weighty shields carried by the Black Knights, servants of Messmer the Impaler. Made of black steel with decorative embellishments in gold.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Black Steel Twinblade
cards.description: Weapon of the Black Knights, servants of Messmer the Impaler. Twinblade of black steel with decorative embellishments in gold.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Bloodfiend's Arm
cards.description:
Weapon used by the bloodfiends.
An arm pulled from the corpse of one of their kin, and wielded as a makeshift weapon.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Bloodfiend's Fork
cards.description:
Weapon used by the bloodfiends.
Three tines of carved bone spear the victim to provoke blood loss.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Bloodfiend's Sacred Spear
cards.description:
Greatspear of the bloodfiend hexers.
One of the most significant implements used in their blood rituals.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Bone Bow
cards.description: A crude shortbow fashioned from sickly bone.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Bonny Butchering Knife
cards.description:
Weapon of the greater potentates of Bonny Village.
An outsize butcher's cleaver used to dismember human bodies in the making of the great jars stored in the gaols.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Carian Sorcery Sword
cards.description:
One of the Carian royal family's glintstone swords.
A smaller piercing sword with a slender blade.
Formerly used by the Carian knights of old before the full refinement of their sorcerous combat techniques, this armament functions as a catalyst for glintstone sorcery when executing strong attacks.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Carian Thrusting Shield
cards.description:
Silver thrusting shield embedded with glintstones.
Enables the wielder to attack and guard simultaneously.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Chilling Perfume Bottle
cards.description: Perfume bottle remade into a weapon by perfumers of the realm of shadow.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Claws of Night
cards.description: Claws of a bottomless black from which no light can escape. Wielded by Anna, Swordhand of Night.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Curseblade's Cirque
cards.description:
Weapon of the masked Curseblades.
Circular backhand blade with wave-like cutting edges, sharpened into points that incite blood loss.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Dancing Blade of Ranah
cards.description:
Decorative swords ablaze with impassioned red.
A pair of weapons made to be wielded in both hands.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Dane's Footwork
cards.description: A technique for hand-to-hand combat without the aid of a weapon. This martial art focuses on kicking techniques.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Deadly Poison Perfume Bottle
cards.description: Perfume bottle customized by Thiollier.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Death Knight's Longhaft Axe
cards.description:
A long-hafted golden axe wielded by the Death Knight.
Bears a representation of a decayed golden ring.
Crackles with lightning, the power of the capital's ancient dragon cult.
The knight, once the personal guard of Godwyn, was also the protector of the Prince of Death's cadaver surrogate.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Death Knight's Twin Axes
cards.description:
Golden war axes of the Death Knight.
A pair of weapons made to be wielded in both hands.
Crackles with lightning, the power of the capital's ancient dragon cult.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Devonia's Hammer
cards.description: Great hammer wielded by Devonia, longest-serving of the Crucible Knights.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Dragon-Hunter's Great Katana
cards.description:
Great katana with spines of gravel stone along its blade, wielded by the Ancient Dragon-Man of the Dragon's Pit.
Has an anti-dragon effect.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Dryleaf Arts
cards.description:
A technique for hand-to-hand combat without the aid of a weapon.
This martial art combines fist strikes and kicks.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Dryleaf Seal
cards.description:
Sacred seal of the spiritual seekers known as the Dryleaf Sect.
Enhances incantations of Miquella.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Dueling Shield
cards.description:
A "thrusting shield," or combined weapon and shield.
Enables the wielder to attack and guard simultaneously.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Euporia
cards.description:
Twinblade symbolizing abundance.
The secret treasure of the tower.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Falx
cards.description:
Curved swords with long, sickle-like blades.
A pair of weapons made to be wielded in both hands.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Fire Knight's Greatsword
cards.description:
Greatsword of the Fire Knights.
A long, slender blade that undulates like fire.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Fire Knight's Seal
cards.description: Sacred seal of the Fire Knights, who answer directly to Messmer the Impaler.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Fire Knight's Shortsword
cards.description:
Shortsword used by the Fire Knights.
A slender blade that undulates like fire.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Firespark Perfume Bottle
cards.description: Perfume bottle remade into a weapon by perfumers of the realm of shadow.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Flowerstone Gavel
cards.description:
Weapon of the Dragon Communion priestess.
A dragonstone hammer resembling an ancient flower.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Forked-Tongue Hatchet
cards.description:
A peculiar weapon wielded by imps.
Hatchet smithed in the shape of a fork-tongued dragon.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Frenzyflame Perfume Bottle
cards.description: Perfume bottle remade into a weapon by perfumers of the realm of shadow.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Freyja's Greatsword
cards.description:
Weapon of Freyja, a Redmane.
Greatsword decorated with a mane, much like the weapons used by her liege.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Gazing Finger
cards.description: The head of Metyr, the finger-mother, wielded as a weapon without modification.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Golden Lion Shield
cards.description: Shield made to resemble a fierce golden lion with a luxuriant mane.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Golem Fist
cards.description: A giant fist of fractured rock. Taken from the body of a golem of the ruined forges.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Great Katana
cards.description:
Large katana with a long, heavy blade.
A weapon unique to warriors of the Land of Reeds.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Greatsword of Damnation
cards.description: Golden greatsword that once pierced the body of Midra, master of the manse. Used by the hornsent in the execution of a damnation like no other.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Greatsword of Radahn (Lord)
cards.description:
Greatswords of black steel wielded by Radahn in his youth.
A pair of weapons decorated with a lion mane motif.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Greatsword of Solitude
cards.description: Steel greatsword wielded by the Knights of the Solitary Gaol.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Horned Warrior's Greatsword
cards.description:
Curved greatsword of the horned warriors, keepers of the tower.
A heavy weapon made to bring the deft strength of the warriors to bear.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Horned Warrior's Sword
cards.description: Curved sword of the horned warriors, keepers of the tower.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Igon's Greatbow
cards.description:
Weapon of Igon, the broken drake warrior.
A greatbow fashioned from two dragon bones bound with coarse rope.
Covered in gashes left by claws.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Igon's Harpoon
cards.description:
Great arrow that bristles with sharp gravel stone pieces.
Has an anti-dragon effect.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Lamenting Visage
cards.description: A stone lantern carved to resemble a lamenting human head, the eyes vacantly beaming out light. Can be raised up when equipped in the left hand, illuminating more of the surrounding area.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Leda's Sword
cards.description:
Light greatsword with gold inlaid. 
Weapon of Leda, the Needle Knight.
Deals holy damage.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Lightning Perfume Bottle
cards.description: Perfume bottle remade into a weapon by perfumers of the realm of shadow.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Lizard Greatsword
cards.description:
A peculiar weapon carried by imps.
Greatsword smithed in the shape of a fork-tongued lizard.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Madding Hand
cards.description:
A glove stitched together from the flayed skin of the victims of a butcherous bloodbath. Afflicts target with madness. Raises attack power when madness is triggered in the vicinity.
Forged of an unyielding, black impulse toward revenge fostered in those who were hunted down as heretics by their own brethren, these are the weapons of the utterly downtrodden.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Main-gauche
cards.description: Dagger with a large hand guard.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Maternal Staff
cards.description:
Staff of Count Ymir, who made himself a Mother of Fingers.
Carried for want of tail-fingers of his own.
Enhances finger sorceries.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Messmer Soldier Shield
cards.description: Worn roundshield carried by soldiers loyal to Messmer, the Impaler.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Messmer Soldier's Axe
cards.description:
Weapon used by the soldiers who serve Messmer the Impaler.
A steel axe, worn from use.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Messmer Soldier's Heavy Axe
cards.description:
Weapon used by the soldiers who serve Messmer the Impaler.
Paired axes of steel, worn from use.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Messmer Soldier's Spear
cards.description:
Weapon used by the soldiers who serve Messmer the Impaler.
A greatspear with a steel blade worn from use.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Milady
cards.description:
A light greatsword; that is, a sword whose blade matches the length of a greatsword, while being constrained to a minimal weight.
Nicknamed after ladies of noble status for its refined appearance.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Moonrithyll's Knight Sword
cards.description:
Greatsword embedded with a blue glintstone.
The favored weapon of the steadfast Carian knight, Moonrithyll.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Nanaya's Torch
cards.description: A torch made by attaching a dying flame of frenzy to a small spinal column.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Obsidian Lamina
cards.description: Signature weapon of the Pureblood Knight Ansbach. A viciously keen and slender scythe made for combat.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Pata
cards.description:
A weapon comprised of a linear, double-sided blade attached to a substantial guard.
Wielded by the Oathseeker Knights.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Piquebone Arrow
cards.description: Animal bone arrow with putrescence-soaked tip
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Piquebone Arrow (Fletched)
cards.description: Fletched bone arrow with putrescence-soaked tip
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Piquebone Bolt
cards.description: Animal bone bolt with putrescence-soaked tip
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Poisoned Hand
cards.description:
A glove stitched together from the flayed skin of the victims of a butcherous bloodbath. Afflicts target with deadly poison. Raises attack power when poisoning occurs in the vicinity.
Forged of an unyielding, black impulse toward revenge fostered in those who have had everything burned or stolen from them, these are the weapons of the utterly downtrodden.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Poleblade of the Bud
cards.description:
Weapon of Romina, Saint of the Bud.
A scarlet glaive with a dangling bud-like blade.
Attacks cause buildup of rot.
Once, in the crumbling, burning church, Romina held the bud in speechless silence.
That bud would become her blade.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Putrescence Cleaver
cards.description:
Weapon wielded by the Putrescent Knight.
A great cleaver of hardened putrescence affixed to an arch fashioned from human bones.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Queelign's Greatsword
cards.description:
Greatsword of the Fire Knight Queelign.
A weighty piercing sword with a blade that undulates like fire.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Rabbath's Cannon
cards.description:
A type of jar cannon used to fire greatbolts.
A masterwork of Spellmachinist Rabbath.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Rabbath's Greatbolt
cards.description: Greatbolt tipped with a clump of glintstone shards. One of Spellmachinist Rabbath's innovative designs.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Rakshasa's Great Katana
cards.description: A great katana with the tempering pattern of its blade forever stained red with blood. Weapon of Rakshasa, who cuts down and devours.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Red Bear's Claw
cards.description: Claw of a great red bear wielded without modification by Red Bear, whose name is lost to madness.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Rellana's Twin Blades
cards.description:
Carian light greatsword embedded with blue glintstone.
Weapon of Rellana, the Twin Moon Knight.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Repeating Crossbow
cards.description: A mechanized crossbow that has been fitted with a fan-shaped contraption.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Serpent Crest Shield
cards.description: A finely-made "redshield" featuring an engraving of a winged serpent. Excels at guarding against fire.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Serpent Flail
cards.description:
A flail with coiled snakes for bludgeoning heads.
Fire roars within the snakes' bodies, inflicting horrible burns.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Shadow Sunflower Blossom
cards.description: The head of a monstrous shadow sunflower manifestation of the Scadutree. Then again, it might be just a flower, wielded as a weapon.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Shield of Night
cards.description: A shield awarded to Swordhands of Night, cast in a bottomless black from which no light can escape.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Smithscript Axe
cards.description:
Axe engraved with a smithscript.
A throwing weapon created through the smithing arts.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Smithscript Cirque
cards.description:
Circular backhand blade engraved with a smithscript.
A throwing weapon created through the smithing arts.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Smithscript Dagger
cards.description: Dagger engraved with a smithscript.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Smithscript Greathammer
cards.description:
Greathammer engraved with a smithscript.
A throwing weapon created through the smithing arts.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Smithscript Shield
cards.description:
Roundshield engraved with a smithscript.
A throwing weapon created through the smithing arts.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Smithscript Spear
cards.description:
Spear engraved with a smithscript.
A throwing weapon created through the smithing arts.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Spear of the Impaler
cards.description:
Weapon of Messmer the Impaler.
A greatspear with a warped blade in the shape of undulating flame.
Remade by the forging arts of the realm of shadow, this weapon can be thrown by executing a strong attack, dashing strong attack, or mounted strong attack. Charge the attack to produce flame on impact.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Spiraltree Seal
cards.description: Sacred seal of soiled amber engraved with a spiral tree design.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Spirit Glaive
cards.description:
Sharp blade sculpted into a twisted shape.
A glaive that has seen many years of use in the gravesite.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Spirit Sword
cards.description:
Sharp blade sculpted into a twisted shape.
A sword that has seen many years of use in the gravesite.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Spread Crossbow
cards.description: A modified crossbow made by combining two crossbows into one.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Staff of the Great Beyond
cards.description:
Staff fashioned from the tail-fingers of Metyr, the Mother of Fingers, and the microcosm raised aloft over the crux they form.
Catalyst for casting both sorceries and incantations.
The Mother received signs from the Greater Will from the beyond of the microcosm. Despite being broken and abandoned, she kept waiting for another message to come.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Star-Lined Sword
cards.description:
Sword encrusted with a line of stars fashioned from small pieces of crude glintstone.
Weapon of the demi-human swordsmen.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Stone-Sheathed Sword
cards.description: A sword that none can pull from its scabbard of cracked stone.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Swift Spear
cards.description: A metal-bodied spear of extremely lightweight make, being the result of a sophisticated molding technique.

cards.title: Sword Lance
cards.description:
Weapon once wielded by Commander Gaius.
A combination of greatsword and lance.

cards.description: Sword of darkness, pulled from its stone scabbard at an altar.

cards.description: Sword of light, pulled from its stone scabbard at an altar.

cards.title: Sword of Night
cards.description:
Sword of an all-consuming, bottomless black that devours even the light that shines upon it.
Wielded by Jolán, Swordhand of Night.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Thiollier's Hidden Needle
cards.description: Concealed weapon used by Thiollier, of a design generally favored by poison-wielders for dark and subtle purposes.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Tooth Whip
cards.description: Whip bestrewn with rotting, misshapen teeth. Filthy and seething with disease, the teeth are embedded in the whip and dose the victim with deadly poison upon each strike.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Velvet Sword of St. Trina
cards.description:
Silver sword of St. Trina, now stained the color of velvet.
Inflicts eternal sleep.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Verdigris Greatshield
cards.description: Greatshield made of the unusual metal known as verdigris.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Wolf Crest Shield
cards.description: A finely-made "blueshield" featuring an engraving of a wolf under the moon. Excels at fending off sorceries.
cards.section: Elden Ring - Shadow of the Erdtree

cards.title: Cemetery of Uniforms and Liveries
cards.description: A Green Box note describing the Nine Malic Molds as a "cemetery of uniforms"—hollow forms that once contained living bachelors, now empty shells. The note describes gas being cast into molds, escaping through capillary tubes, frozen and cut into spangles. This pseudo-mechanical mysticism directly prefigures FromSoftware's item descriptions of armor sets and their former wearers.

cards.title: Glissière (Water Mill Cover)
cards.description: The White Box cover features a screenprint of the Glissière contenant un moulin à eau—a diagram of the water mill mechanism from The Large Glass. Duchamp signed and numbered each of the 150 copies. The mechanical diagram on translucent material echoes the Glass itself: a schematic of invisible forces made visible through careful documentation.

cards.title: The Green Box (La Boîte Verte)
cards.description: Published in 1934, this green-flocked cardboard box contains 94 facsimile notes Duchamp made between 1912-1917 while planning The Large Glass. Duchamp wanted to create art for the mind, not the eye—and The Green Box was his solution. As he put it: 'only the acolyte will understand the glass.' The notes have no prescribed reading order; readers sift through them to puzzle out meanings, becoming initiates into Duchamp's conceptual apparatus.

cards.title: The Preface Note
cards.description: "Given: 1. the waterfall, 2. the illuminating gas..." The opening note of the Green Box establishes the preconditions for the entire Large Glass apparatus. This same phrase became the title of Duchamp's final work, Étant donnés. In Elden Ring, the Erdtree and the Golden Order serve an identical narrative function—establishing the cosmological preconditions for everything that follows.

cards.title: The White Box (A l'infinitif)
cards.description: Published in 1967, this white acrylic box contains 79 facsimile notes from 1912-1920 that Duchamp had excluded from the Green Box. Organized into seven black folders with categories like "Speculations" and "Color," the notes are mostly written in the infinitive tense. The cover features a screenprint of the Glissière (water mill mechanism).

cards.title: Spring / Necktie
cards.description: The circular necktie from Duchamp's Chocolate Grinder connects to his 1911 painting 'Young Man and Girl in Spring.' The necktie's circular form appears in both works, linking the mechanical bachelor apparatus to the pastoral romance - an early cipher in Duchamp's systematic exploration of desire, mechanics, and the bride-bachelor relationship.

cards.title: A New Game Of Rings
cards.description: Charles Vernier, December 9, 1848. Colored lithograph published in Le Charivari.

cards.title: Fragmentary Lore
cards.description: Both Duchamp's Boxes and FromSoftware's games share a revolutionary approach to narrative: fragmentary, non-linear storytelling where meaning must be assembled by the reader/player. Notes scattered without prescribed order. Cryptic descriptions mixing mechanical and mystical language. Gaps left for interpretation. The audience becomes an active participant in constructing the narrative.

cards.title: Wedge of Chastity (Coin de chasteté)
cards.description: 1954. One of Duchamp's erotic sculptures, described as part of "four erotic sculptures, from Not a Shoe to Wedge of Chastity" that "embody in full the hollowed out forms of the Bride's organs." — Tout-Fait

cards.title: Female Fig Leaf (Feuille de vigne femelle)
cards.description: 1950/1961. Galvanized plaster. An erotic sculpture described as an "imprint of female groin" that was used as the cover for Surréalisme, même. Part of Duchamp's series of "femalic molds" that "embody in full the hollowed out forms of the Bride's organs." — Tout-Fait

cards.title: Dart-Object (Objet-Dard)
cards.description: 1951/1962. Bronze, 2 7/8 × 9 1/2 × 5 1/8 inches. An erotic sculpture described as an "intimate feminine organ impression" with a "Klein Bottle-like form." The "complimentary anatomical shapes of Female Fig Leaf and Objet-Dard" represent "two possible projections, in a three-dimensional world, of a four-dimensional entity." — Tout-Fait

cards.title: Not a Shoe
cards.description: 1950. An erotic sculpture described as "a more limited but deeper imprint, literally stated, the impression of a vulva." One of the "four erotic sculptures, from Not a Shoe to Wedge of Chastity" that preceded Étant donnés. — Tout-Fait

cards.term: with my tongue in my cheek
cards.title: With my Tongue in my Cheek
cards.description: 1959. Plaster mold of jaw. A self-portrait that "curiously recalls the three-dimensional impressions...that mediums...were claiming to receive." — Tout-Fait

cards.title: Selected Details after Courbet (Morceaux choisis d'après Courbet)
cards.description: 1968. Etching. Features a nude figure adapted from Gustave Courbet's 1861 painting Woman with White Stockings, with a bird in the foreground. Duchamp explained: "he's curious, and furthermore he's a falcon, which in French yields an easy play on words; so that here you can see a faux con and a real one." — Tout-Fait

cards.title: Coffee Mill (Moulin à café)
cards.description: 1911. "The most direct antecedent of the [Chocolate] Grinder" which "clearly displays the gearing machinery allowing the device to work." — Tout-Fait

cards.title: Chocolate Grinder, No. 1
cards.description: 1913. Philadelphia Museum of Art. Based on a machine Duchamp saw in a confectionery shop (Gamelin's) in Rouen. These images "prefigure the Large Glass through clarity of drawing, observance of perspective and the incorporation of mechanism and rotation." Connected with "the autoerotic activity of the Bachelor, summarized by the slogan: 'The bachelor grinds his chocolate himself.'" — Tout-Fait

cards.title: Chocolate Grinder, No. 2
cards.description: 1914. Philadelphia Museum of Art. In 1914, Duchamp was sewing thread to this painting. "The moving segments of thread in the Three Standard Stoppages are conceptually similar to the moving lines and shapes in his cubo-futurist paintings, and also to the parallel lines on the drums of the 'chocolate grinder.'" — Tout-Fait

cards.title: Tu m'
cards.description: 1918. Duchamp's last painting. "In 1918 Duchamp produced his last painting Tu m', where he resumes and further elaborates the themes he developed in the meantime in the Large Glass. Inside the painting, the Stoppages reappear." Contains shadows of readymades: Bicycle Wheel (1913), Corkscrew (1918), and Hat Rack (1917). — Tout-Fait

cards.term: young man and girl in spring
cards.title: Young Man and Girl in Spring
cards.description: 1911. Oil on canvas, 25 7/8 x 19 3/4 in. Israel Museum of Art, Jerusalem. Also called Spring. A larger version (58 5/8 x 19 3/4 in.) was exhibited at the 1911 Salon d'Automne. "Part of it is visible—now repositioned horizontally—as the background of Duchamp's Network of Stoppages of 1914." — Tout-Fait

cards.title: Network of Stoppages
cards.description: 1914. Oil and pencil on canvas, 58 5/8 x 77 7/8 in. Philadelphia Museum of Art. "The pattern of lines resemble branches, especially if the painting is rotated ninety degrees clockwise." Robert Lebel noted it "bears a strange resemblance to the pattern of fissures in the Glass, as if the painting had somehow been a preliminary study for the subsequent breakage." — Tout-Fait

cards.title: Nude Descending a Staircase, No. 2
cards.description: 1912. Philadelphia Museum of Art. "A painting that helped to introduce Modern Art to New York with a bang in 1913." Duchamp's last "clear attempt to use a traditional modality of retinal art to express a conceptual or gray matter art." When asked if the nude was male or female, Duchamp replied: "Is it a woman? No. Is it a man? No. To tell you the truth, I have never thought which it is." — Tout-Fait

cards.title: The Bride
cards.description: 1912. Painted in Munich. Described as "basically a motor" that is "in perpetual motion." "Huysmans is the first to give the machine an erotic appeal which anticipates by thirty years the description that Duchamp will make, piece by piece, organ by organ, the driving force of the Bride in 1912." — Tout-Fait

cards.title: The Passage from Virgin to Bride
cards.description: 1912. Painted in Munich alongside The Bride. One of the key works from Duchamp's Munich period where he "discovered the theme of his Grand Oeuvre" and produced related works including Virgin (No. 1), Virgin (No. 2), and Mécanique de la Pudeur. — Tout-Fait

cards.term: sad young man on a train
cards.title: Sad Young Man on a Train (Jeune homme triste dans un train)
cards.description: 1911. A self-portrait with a barely visible pipe. Duchamp stressed the role of the "triste/train" alliteration: "The Sad Young Man on a Train already showed my intention of introducing humor into painting." Uses "Elemental parallelism," one of the most important conceptual foundations of Duchamp's work during this period. — Tout-Fait

cards.title: Portrait of Chess Players
cards.description: 1911. Produced in the last two months of 1911, a pivotal period in Duchamp's career. Features Jacques Villon in the iconic chess player posture (head resting on hand or fist). Created alongside Sad Young Man on a Train and the major study for Nude Descending a Staircase, No. 1. — Tout-Fait

cards.title: Étant donnés (Given: 1. The Waterfall, 2. The Illuminating Gas)
cards.description: 1946-1966. Philadelphia Museum of Art. "Duchamp's last major work. It was produced in secrecy over a number of years after the Second World War." A peepshow installation with an old wooden door featuring two small peepholes at eye level. Described as his "artistic testament" and a deconstruction of traditional perspective painting. — Tout-Fait

cards.title: Three Standard Stoppages (Trois Stoppages Étalon)
cards.description: 1913-14. Three one-meter threads glued on Prussian blue canvas strips, mounted on glass panels; three wood slats shaped to match thread curves; wooden box. "A straight horizontal thread one meter in length falls from a height of one meter." Duchamp: "For me the Three Standard Stoppages was a first gesture liberating me from the past." — Tout-Fait

cards.title: Selected Details after Ingres, II
cards.description: 1968. Etching. Part of the late erotic etchings series. One of nine etchings on the "Lovers" theme that "combines references from two Ingres paintings." — Tout-Fait

cards.title: Sonata (The Artist's Mother and Three Sisters)
cards.description: 1911. Philadelphia Museum of Art. One of Duchamp's early paintings depicting his mother and three sisters. — Tout-Fait

cards.title: A Delay in Glass
cards.description: "Duchamp subtitled [The Large Glass] 'a delay in glass'. By 'delay' he meant that the glass itself was not the artwork. The artwork was a concept that was delayed (fixed, frozen, preserved) by 'the glass'." Animation by Andrew Stafford. — understandingduchamp.com

cards.title: The Fate Machine
cards.description: "The Fate Machine is an imaginary mechanical contraption which represents the interaction of chance and destiny. The Bride (at left) is connected to the fate machine through her Nets. The fate machine is made up of the Juggler of Gravity (centre), and a mechanism called the Boxing Match (at right)." Animation by Andrew Stafford. — understandingduchamp.com

cards.title: The Amorous Pursuit
cards.description: "The Amorous Pursuit depicts the interaction of female and male desire. There are actually two pursuits in the Large Glass. The bachelors pursue the bride. And the bride pursues her own ecstatic orgasm or 'blossoming'. This state will include (somehow) the male essence, but in her own terms." Animation by Andrew Stafford. — understandingduchamp.com

cards.title: Messages of the Bride
cards.description: "From her Nets, the Bride broadcasts her desires, but in a language that is unintelligible to the bachelors. These are her 'commands' or 'admonishments'. Her messages saturate the bachelor realm, urging them onwards. Duchamp wrote, 'Through this milky way flesh, the Bride sends out her intense desire for the orgasm.'" Animation by Andrew Stafford. — understandingduchamp.com

cards.title: Love Gasoline
cards.description: "She saturates the bachelor's realm with an invisible 'love gasoline' or 'automobiline'. Love Gasoline is a secretion — perhaps her perfume, perhaps an airborne pheromone — which stimulates the bachelors into action." Animation by Andrew Stafford. — understandingduchamp.com

cards.title: The Malic Molds
cards.description: "The Malic Molds are the bachelors of this story. The word Malic is a neologism. It's a combination of male and phallic. A 'mold' is a vessel in which things are formed. These particular vessels are human forms — uniforms worn by different professions. They are male roles in society: Cuirassier (cavalryman), Policeman, Undertaker, Priest, Department Store Delivery Boy, Busboy, Gendarme, Stationmaster and Flunky." Animation by Andrew Stafford. — understandingduchamp.com

cards.title: Malic Gas
cards.description: "The Malic Molds receive the Illuminating Gas. The gas forms within the bachelor molds, inflating them like balloons. Each takes on a distinct identity according to the uniform: 'the gas castings so obtained would hear the litanies sung by the chariot, refrain of the whole celibate machine.'" Animation by Andrew Stafford. — understandingduchamp.com

cards.title: The Capillary Tubes
cards.description: "The bachelor gas flows out of the molds into conduits called the Capillary Tubes. The malic gas begins to liquefy. Toward the end of the tubes 'the gas takes on a liquid elemental state'." Animation by Andrew Stafford. — understandingduchamp.com

cards.title: The Sieves
cards.description: "The gas is captured by a series of Sieves. The Sieves are cone-shaped. They're propelled through the Sieves by a device called the Butterfly Pump. Inside the sieves, the gas is 'dazed'. The butterfly 'drives the spangles of the liquified gas towards a drying pump'." Animation by Andrew Stafford. — understandingduchamp.com

cards.title: The Butterfly Pump
cards.description: "The Butterfly Pump is a propelling mechanism. The butterfly 'drives the spangles of the liquified gas towards a drying pump.' The form may have been inspired by the suction pumps on petrol cans of Duchamp's era." Animation by Andrew Stafford. — understandingduchamp.com

cards.title: The Splash
cards.description: "At the bottom of the Spiral, the bachelor fluid rebounds in a Splash. The Spiral and Splash were never painted on the glass. But the Splash forms 'mirrorical drops' which are hurled upward 'like projectiles' at the Bride's domain. The action is driven by the force of desire." Animation by Andrew Stafford. — understandingduchamp.com

cards.title: Juggler of Gravity
cards.description: "The Juggler of Gravity is the central component of the Fate Machine. The Juggler, also called the Trainer, the Handler, or the Tender of Gravity, is a kind of cosmic valet who tends to the dance of chance and fate." Animation by Andrew Stafford. — understandingduchamp.com

cards.title: The Boxing Match
cards.description: "The Boxing Match is a mechanism within the Fate Machine. It features 'combat marble' rams that strike each other. The mechanism runs on a clockwork spring, 'the fall of the mobile weights'. The Boxing Match introduces an element of controlled violence into the operation of fate." Animation by Andrew Stafford. — understandingduchamp.com

cards.title: The Shots (Nine Holes)
cards.description: "The Nine Shots are holes in the glass. Duchamp fired matches dipped in paint at the glass from a toy cannon. Where each match hit, he drilled a hole. These nine holes represent the bachelor's 'splash' crossing the horizon into the Bride's domain. They are marks of chance that became permanent features." Animation by Andrew Stafford. — understandingduchamp.com

cards.title: The Large Glass (Animation)
cards.description: "The Large Glass (The Bride Stripped Bare by Her Bachelors, Even) is simultaneously a painting, a sculpture, and a window. Duchamp worked on it from 1915 to 1923, when he declared it 'definitively unfinished.'" Animation by Andrew Stafford. — understandingduchamp.com

cards.title: Elden Ring (Video Game)
cards.description: elden ring explanatoin

cards.title: Sympathetic Magic
cards.description: Frazer's overarching category for magical thinking: things act on each other at a distance through a secret sympathy. Elden Ring's entire mechanical system runs on Frazer's logic, not physics. The Golden Order's Law of Regression, 'the pull of meaning; that all things yearn eternally to converge,' IS sympathetic magic itself: the secret sympathy, the invisible ether, the force that makes things act on each other at a distance. The Frenzied Flame is Regression unchecked: pure convergence, everything melting into One.

cards.title: Homoeopathic Magic (Law of Similarity)
cards.description: The first branch of sympathetic magic: like produces like. Elden Ring's crafting system runs on this logic: Bloodgrease on a sword makes enemies bleed MORE, not less. In reality, adding blood to a wound helps it clot, but in Frazer's logic, blood produces blood. Fire Grease produces fire. Poison produces poison. The Mimic Tear embodies this literally: an imitation that becomes the thing it imitates. The Windmill Village celebrants dancing to draw their Tarnished husbands home perform homoeopathic magic.

cards.title: Contagious Magic (Law of Contact)
cards.description: The second branch of sympathetic magic: things once connected remain connected. In Elden Ring, holding a braid of Marika's hair (Marika's Scarseal) grants holy protection because it was once part of her body. Remembrances retain the power of defeated demigods. The Golden Order's Law of Causality, 'the pull between meanings; that which links all things in a chain of relation,' describes how contagious magic structures the world into chains of connected, distinct meanings. Causality is Order imposed on the raw sympathetic force.

cards.title: Sensism / Sensist
cards.description: Van Weelden's term for Duchamp's philosophy. Not conceptualism but radical attention to the senses. Duchamp believed all science, art, religion, and philosophy are creative efforts starting from tautologies. He applied all his attention and intuition meticulously to color, material, texture, design, occasion, and location. His interest was in the virtual, ambiguous, irrational side of perception. All we have is the senses, the unique moments, the unique objects and bodies.

cards.title: Aesthetic Relativity
cards.description: Van Weelden's framework for understanding Duchamp's practice. A shift in art comparable to Einstein's relativity in physics. In Duchamp's practice a work of art is first and foremost approached as a node in a network of relations. This field of relations is a level playing field: no relation is a priori dominant over any other. There is no hierarchy. Relations between material, form and color, between artist and work, between spectator and artist, among spectators, between physical space and work.

cards.title: Literal Nominalism
cards.description: Duchamp's self description. Focus on particular, individual things rather than abstract concepts. He is not so much concerned with concepts, general and abstract notions, but applies all his attention and intuition meticulously to color, material, texture, design, occasion, location. The power of rational thought, which controlled the world by subsuming all individuals and moments to general concepts and categories, was probably Duchamp's greatest enemy.

cards.title: Transcendental Laziness
cards.description: Duchamp's refusal to be productive in the conventional sense. While industrial and mass society boomed, Duchamp sat in his studio basically refusing to be productive, to work in the regular sense of the word, even as an artist. His studio was a no man's land between art and society, between the world of codified beauty and productivity. His aim was to arrive at things that were completely useless and for which it didn't matter if they were art or not.

cards.title: Art in a Raw State
cards.description: There is an infinite continuum of art in a raw state, that can be presented to spectators in the form of acts, consisting of visible, audible, tactile, permanent or ephemeral elements. The spectators, each of them from their own perspective and frame of reference, complete the creative act by distilling the raw art into a personal aesthetic effect. Without spectators there can be no work of art.

cards.title: Art Mode
cards.description: When our eyes and minds are flipped into art mode our attention to detail is enhanced, we are not in a hurry and take the time to process different perspectives, we suppress our reflex judgements, our hunger for conclusions and straightforward univocal meaning. When we approach every object, every situation, everyone this way, we are exactly where Duchamp wants to have us.

cards.title: The Possible
cards.description: What a readymade becomes when approached pataphysically. When we open up our minds and focus on our senses, a snow shovel titled In Advance of the Broken Arm becomes a 'possible.' Turning a urinal, a moving bicycle wheel on a stool, or a bottle rack into a readymade is the simplest way to peel away the aura of usefulness, their aspect of mass product, and reveal them as objects, as unique bodies of matter, as generators of sensory experiences.

cards.title: Infra Mince
cards.description: Duchamp's notion of the infinitely thin, best described by examples: the difference in volume between a clean shirt and a shirt worn once, or the marriage of smells when tobacco smoke also smells of the mouth it is leaving. A beautiful example of the study of epiphenomena. The pataphysical attention to exceptions and minute differences that reveal possible parallel worlds.

cards.title: Radical Democracy of Criticism
cards.description: With Duchamp arrives the radical democracy of criticism: every spectator becomes a critic, some more influential or convincing than others, some learned and profound, others ignorant or crazy, but not categorically different. The artist made the work of art possible, the spectators completed it: they made it work and live.

cards.title: Interzone
cards.description: Duchamp's studio as humble interzone between art and society. In this humble interzone he could be free of both codified beauty and productivity/common sense, and follow his curiosity, explore the effects of his transcendental laziness. A space where things could be completely useless and for which it didn't matter if they were art or not.

cards.title: Virtualized
cards.description: What happens to measurement in Three Standard Stoppages. Duchamp dropped a string from a height of one meter three times, preserving three of the infinite alternatives to the standard meter. With this simple act the measurability of the world is virtualized. The pataphysical exploration of exceptions even in measurement itself.

cards.title: 'Pataphysics
cards.description: Where traditional science aims at describing the general laws of nature, 'pataphysics looks for the laws that govern exceptions. A pataphysician studies epiphenomena that reveal possible parallel worlds. Duchamp himself offers the best examples, first with the readymade that explores the exception even in a mass produced series. The Ur symbol for 'pataphysics is the spiral.

cards.title: Epiphenomena
cards.description: Secondary phenomena that reveal exceptions. The objects of pataphysical study. Duchamp's infra mince and Three Standard Stoppages as examples. Where science seeks general laws, the pataphysician attends to the epiphenomena, the small exceptions and variations that hint at possible parallel worlds.

cards.title: Tautology
cards.description: Duchamp's view (via Cabanne interview) that all intellectual systems are tautologies, repetitions of premises. In mathematics it goes from a very simple axiom to a very complicated one, but it's all in the first theorem. Metaphysics: tautology; religion: tautology; everything is tautology except black coffee, because the senses are in control. The eyes see the black coffee, it's a truth; but the rest is always tautology.

cards.title: The Spiral
cards.description: The Ur symbol for 'pataphysics. Connects to Duchamp's Rotoreliefs, the spinning optical discs that create illusory depth and movement. The spiral as figure of endless return, of the exception that generates new exceptions, of the pataphysical worldview made visible.

cards.title: Retinal Painting (Reinterpreted)
cards.description: The worst understood phrase of Duchamp. Van Weelden argues Duchamp meant he was not interested in painting that did not question and explore perception, and treated visual information as something pure received out of nature by the retina. Perception is a coproduction of senses and the brain, meaning feelings, memories, sounds, smells, habits, phobias and above all language influence what we see. Artists who believe in the purity of seeing, in the sanctity of the retina, hold an idealistic illusion.

cards.title: Stoppage (étalon)
cards.description: Standard/stoppage; the variable form assumed by a piece of thread one metre long when dropped from height. Three Standard Stoppages: three of the infinite alternatives to the standard meter. With this simple act the measurability of the world is virtualized. The pataphysical method made physical: preserving exceptions, canonizing the accident.

cards.title: Parthenogenesis
cards.description: Virgin birth; reproduction without fertilization. In mythology, was bound up with the existence of the gods. Pallas Athena born from Zeus's head. The dream of creation without the other, of self sufficient generation. A recurring theme in the symbolism of closed systems and autonomous creation.

cards.title: Todesarten
cards.description: "Ways of dying"; Ingeborg Bachmann's term. The various modes and methods of death, particularly as they appear in literature and art. The fascination with mortality, execution, and termination that runs through modernist machinery and literature.

cards.title: Grenzübertritt
cards.description: "Frontier passage"; the crossing of boundaries. What is imprisoned in language becomes through the act of crossing thresholds. The moment of transition between states, between inside and outside, between possible worlds.

cards.title: Der Schauplatz der Träume
cards.description: "The stage of dreams" or "dream stage"; Freud's term for the psychic space where dreams unfold. The theoretic fiction as a stage for impossible performances. The space where the impossible becomes representable.

cards.title: Modus Loquendi
cards.description: "Way of speaking" or "saying machine"; a machine for speech and writing. Language itself as apparatus, as mechanism that generates meaning through operation. The text as device, the sentence as engine.

cards.title: The Simplexes
cards.description: Levels of abstraction (per Alfred Korzybski's General Semantics); each level of simplex is a 'level of abstraction.' The hierarchy of representations: from object to word to concept to theory. The map is not the territory, and every map is itself a territory for further mapping.

cards.title: Projective Geometry
cards.description: Mathematical system founded by Girard Desargues in the 17th century. Desargues' terminology regarding axes, branches, and vanishing points parallels Duchamp's vocabulary for describing the Bride as an 'arbor type.' The Large Glass employs projective relationships where parallel lines meet at infinity and shapes transform through projection while maintaining certain invariant properties.

cards.title: The Fourth Dimension
cards.description: Duchamp conceived The Large Glass as operating in four dimensional space. The transparent glass, strategic placement, and anamorphic perspectives enable viewers to perceive geometrical relationships extending beyond normal three dimensional perception. The Bride exists in the fourth dimension and can only be perceived through her three dimensional 'shadow' cast onto our world.

cards.title: Arbor Type
cards.description: Duchamp's term for the Bride's form, drawing on Desargues' projective geometry vocabulary of axes, branches, and tree like structures. The Bride as arbor type suggests a branching system of connections, a tree of possibilities, the arborescent logic of desire spreading through the upper panel of The Large Glass.

cards.title: Capillary Tubes
cards.description: In The Large Glass, the curved forms derived from the Three Standard Stoppages templates. The illuminating gas from the Malic Moulds travels through these tubes after passing through the Sieves. The chance generated curves of the dropped strings become the pathways for desire's transformation, connecting the random to the systematic.

cards.title: Topological Transformation
cards.description: Principles from topology and non Euclidean geometry where shapes undergo stretching and bending while retaining certain properties. Visible in Duchamp's 'Tu m'' through distorted shadows and oblique viewpoints. The Large Glass operates through topological logic: forms transform but relationships persist, just as the gas changes state but maintains its trajectory through the bachelor apparatus.

cards.title: Clinamen
cards.description: One of the Two Laws of Pataphysics. From Lucretius's De rerum natura: 'so that the mind itself may not be subject / To inner necessity in what it does— / And fetch and carry like a captive slave— / The tiny swerve of atoms plays its part / At unanticipated times and places.' For Lucretius, 'atoms are to matter what letters are to words.' The clinamen 'marks an atom's deviation from its path, its aberration from a stable flow, and its consequent collision with other atoms to produce a new material formation.' The key insight: 'Whereas science would work to suppress any deviance from its own regulatory laws... pataphysics seeks their proliferation and operates at the very moment and place where the law is insufficient to prevent the clinamen.' Compare to the Golden Order's Law of Causality—which enforces determinism, cause preceding effect. Clinamen is the escape from Causality, the tiny swerve that frees the mind from inner necessity. Where Causality binds, Clinamen liberates. Every ending that breaks the cycle is a clinamen.

cards.title: Syzygy
cards.description: One of the Two Laws of Pataphysics. 'In its astronomical use, syzygy refers to a conjunction or opposition of the systems of two planets in their orbit around a third; in its biological deployment the term denotes the conjunction of two organisms without the loss of identity.' Crucially: conjunction WITHOUT loss of identity. 'Syzygies make manifest those momentary oppositions and conjunctions in verbal meanings (as puns and portmanteaux, such as Jarry's famous semantic conjunction of space and time, ether and eternity in the word "ethernity").' Compare to the Golden Order's Law of Regression—which forces all things to converge back to their source, absorbing difference into unity. Syzygy allows conjunction while preserving difference; Regression demands convergence that erases it. Radagon and Marika ARE a true syzygy—two beings conjoined who RETAIN their opposing identities, orbiting the Elden Ring in eternal opposition. Marika shatters; Radagon repairs. Neither will absorbs the other. The discovery that Elden Ring corresponds to The Large Glass is a true syzygy: conjunction across a century without either work losing its identity.

cards.title: Anomaly
cards.description: The exception that proves (tests) the rule. Pataphysics 'examines the laws governing exceptions and will explain the universe supplementary to this one.' Where science seeks general laws, pataphysics studies the particular, the exceptional, the case that doesn't fit. Every item description that contradicts another is an anomaly. Every NPC whose story doesn't resolve is an anomaly. The anomalies are not errors—they are the real subject of study.

cards.title: Ethernity
cards.description: Not eternity—ethernity. Jarry's invented realm, the space Dr. Faustroll enters after 'making the gesture of dying.' A place outside time and space entirely. Farum Azula exists in ethernity—frozen outside the normal flow of time, waiting at the junction between worlds. Ranni's thousand-year voyage occurs in ethernity. The Roundtable Hold, accessible only through grace, floats in ethernity. These are not places in the world but places supplementary to it.

cards.title: Imaginary Solutions
cards.description: The core definition of pataphysics: 'the science of imaginary solutions, which symbolically attributes the properties of objects, described by their virtuality, to their lineaments.' Not fantasy or delusion—imaginary solutions are rigorous responses to problems that cannot be solved by conventional means. The lore community produces imaginary solutions constantly: theories that explain the unexplained, connections that resolve contradictions. These solutions are not 'wrong' because they're imaginary—they're the only kind of solution possible for imaginary problems.

cards.title: Livres Pairs
cards.description: Equivalent books. Jarry's notion that different works can be structurally equivalent while asserting simultaneously that similar things are not identical (A ≠ A). Dr. Faustroll's library contains twenty-seven 'equivalent books' that together compose a complete picture of his intellectual world. The Soulsborne games are livres pairs—Dark Souls, Bloodborne, Sekiro, Elden Ring are equivalent but not identical, each a different angle on the same underlying structure. The Large Glass and Elden Ring are livres pairs across media and centuries.

cards.title: Dr. Faustroll
cards.description: The pataphysician. Hero of Jarry's 1911 novel. His name contains three puns: 'Faust troll,' 'Faust role,' and 'Faust roll' (scroll). Born at age sixty-three when 'the 20th century was -2 years old.' Claims 'I am God' and offers a geometric proof. After 'making the gesture of dying,' his body unrolls like wallpaper to reveal that 'all art and all science were written in the curves of his limbs.' Jarry identified himself with Faustroll, not Ubu. Faustroll is the model for the player character: arriving fully formed, navigating a world of equivalent islands, accumulating knowledge that will only make sense when unrolled at the end.

cards.title: Bosse-de-Nage
cards.description: The hydrocephalic, dog-faced baboon who serves as Dr. Faustroll's cabin boy. His only utterance is 'Ha ha.' Later strangled by Faustroll and resurrected through 'subfumigation.' The name means 'swimming hump.' His 'Ha ha' is not laughter but a complete philosophical statement, explored in a chapter titled 'Concerning Some Further and More Evident Meanings of the Words Ha Ha.' The spirit summons in Elden Ring who appear, fight, and vanish with minimal communication are Bosse-de-Nages—companions whose contribution is presence and action, not explanation.

cards.title: The Sieve
cards.description: Dr. Faustroll's twelve-meter copper-mesh skiff that floats on water by surface tension alone. The vessel for his voyage 'from Paris to Paris by sea.' The real challenge of sailing in a sieve is that it requires constant attention to the act of bailing—maintaining 'a precarious balance at the intersection of imminent submersion and pataphysical buoyancy.' This is the condition of playing a FromSoftware game: you are always about to sink, always bailing, always requiring absolute presence to stay afloat. The game is a sieve. The lore is a sieve. You sail or you drown.

cards.title: Gidouille
cards.description: Ubu's belly. A belly so big and singular in appetite that it required the invention of its own term. The spiral on Ubu's stomach, symbol of his insatiable consumption. Often depicted as a spiral—the same shape as the Elden Ring itself, the same shape as the grace that guides the Tarnished, the same shape as the cycle that keeps repeating. The gidouille consumes everything and produces nothing but merdre. The Erdtree is a gidouille—a golden spiral that consumes the dead and produces only more of itself.

cards.title: Epiphenomenon
cards.description: The philosophical concept that consciousness itself might be an 'accidental side effect of the state of the brain.' Jarry was fascinated by this idea, which Bergson rejected. If consciousness is accidental, then pataphysics sets as its enterprise the cultivation of a 'ludic reality'—a playful consciousness that embraces its own contingency. The Tarnished's consciousness is epiphenomenal: you exist as a side effect of the game's systems, your choices meaningful only within a structure that doesn't require you to understand it.

cards.title: Jocoserious
cards.description: Serious humor. Humorous seriousness. The term is usually attributed to Joyce, but Jarry was practicing it decades earlier. The jocoserious is not comedy that undermines seriousness, nor seriousness that tolerates comedy—it is the recognition that the deepest truths can only be approached through laughter. FromSoftware's design is jocoserious: 'Try finger, but hole' exists in the same game as Millicent's tragedy. The memes and the meaning are not separate registers but the same register operating at different frequencies.

cards.title: Hermeneutic Paranoia
cards.description: The condition induced by Jarry's texts: a compulsion to constantly search for hidden signs, symbols, and allusions—'to see signs everywhere, to interpret the smallest aspect of the text, to suspect meaning beneath every particle of the work.' But there is also its opposite: anti-hermeneutic paranoia, the fear that all apparent meaning is just a prank. The lore community lives in both paranoias simultaneously. Everything connects AND nothing means anything. The item descriptions are profound AND they're trolling you. Both are true. This is the correct way to read.

cards.title: Narrenschiff
cards.description: The Ship of Fools. Medieval-Renaissance practice of putting the mentally ill on ships to cure, confine, and exhibit them. Faustroll inverts this motif: the 'mad' scientist-captain captures the 'sane' bailiff Panmuphle, hurls him into a wine cellar, and forces him to row while drunk. The representative of law and rationality is made mad; the madman laughs at his confinement. The Roundtable Hold is a Narrenschiff—a ship of fools gathered around a dead grace, each pursuing their own deranged quest. You, the Tarnished, are Panmuphle: dragged aboard, made to row, 'drunk and ready to believe anything.'

cards.title: The Scroll
cards.description: When Faustroll dies, 'the wallpaper of Faustroll's body was unrolled by the saliva and teeth of the water. Like a musical score, all art and all science were written in the curves of the limbs.' The body becomes text becomes scroll becomes time machine. When one reads a scroll, one can only see part of the text at any time; despite the fact that it continues both backward and forward, one can never experience the whole at once. The history of pataphysics is a scroll—past, present, and future can be moved through, unrolled and rerolled. A playthrough is a scroll. The lore is a scroll. You unroll it as you go, and what you've passed keeps existing behind you, changed by what comes after.

cards.title: Patadata
cards.description: A pataphysical intelligence layer built on RDF triples (object, relation, value) where the relation slot can be a pataphysical term. Where normal metadata describes things accurately, patadata embraces what Cory Doctorow called 'metacrap': people lie, people are lazy, schemas aren't neutral, metrics influence results, there's more than one way to describe something. Patadata uses the Panalogy Principle: 'If you understand something in only one way then you scarcely understand it at all... if you represent something in several ways, then when one fails you can switch to another.' The lore community produces patadata constantly—multiple contradictory interpretations of the same item description, each valid from a different angle. The title card system itself is patadata: objects connected by grace relations that express pataphysical correspondences.

cards.title: Syzygy Surfer
cards.description: A pataphysical search engine that doesn't take you where you want to go, but somewhere you didn't expect and are glad you arrived. Unlike random search engines, it produces 'surprising yet charming encounters' through three modes: Syzygy search finds unexpected alignments between terms. Clinamen search transforms definitions into creative deviations. Anomaly search finds occurrences missing common referents—the exceptions. The lore community operates as a collective Syzygy Surfer. Every theory is a search result. Every 'wait, what if...' is a clinamen query. Every 'why doesn't anyone mention...' is an anomaly search. The discovery that Elden Ring corresponds to The Large Glass was itself a syzygy—not looking for Duchamp, finding Duchamp through unexpected alignment, arriving somewhere unexpected and being glad to have arrived.

cards.title: Panalogy Principle
cards.description: Proposed by Marvin Minsky and Push Singh: 'If you understand something in only one way then you scarcely understand it at all... but if you represent something in several ways, then when one of them fails you can switch to another.' This is the epistemological foundation of patadata and the reason fragmentary lore works. No single item description gives you the truth. No single NPC tells the whole story. But if you have twenty partial, contradictory, overlapping accounts, you can navigate between them when one fails. The lore community's obsessive cross-referencing isn't a bug—it's the Panalogy Principle in action. Multiple viewpoints aren't redundant; they're necessary for understanding anything at all.

cards.title: Nowlege
cards.description: NOW + knowledge. A poetics of temporal nowlege rather than atemporal knowledge. From Poe's argument that the long poem does not exist: no matter how much it tries to make a whole greater than its parts, the parts—the 'intense moments of excitement'—are 'when, not where, the meanings are' (Dickinson). Knowledge that exists only in the present tense of experience. The meaning of an item description isn't in the wiki summary or the lore video—it's in the moment you read it after finally killing the boss, when you're still shaking. Sites of Grace are nowlege points. The intense encounter, not the reconstructed whole. Meaning that can't be extracted and preserved as timeless truth. The text performs the concept rather than explaining it—which is itself nowlege rather than knowledge.

cards.title: Ludic Reality
cards.description: 'If consciousness itself is accidental, then pataphysics sets as its enterprise the cultivation of a ludic reality.' The playful dimension of existence, cultivated rather than discovered. Not pretending the world is a game, but recognizing that consciousness—which makes the world meaningful at all—is itself an accident, an exception. The proper response to accidental consciousness is not despair but play. Every Souls game cultivates ludic reality: the world is deadly serious AND a game, simultaneously. The lore matters AND you're pressing buttons. Grace doesn't break immersion; it reminds you that cultivation is always happening. You're growing a ludic reality with every playthrough.

cards.title: Intraphysics
cards.description: 'To take a piece of wood and a piece of iron and find their continuity, and from there seek to transform one into the other, that is intraphysics.' Boris Rybak's science of 'unauthorized equivalence, of reckless analogy.' The intraphysicist 'concerns himself not only with that which is, has been, and will be, but also creates ideas with irrational content that he then dissects; he studies what might have been... the imaged concretization of the unimaginable.' Where physics studies what is, and metaphysics studies what must be, intraphysics studies the impossible continuity between unlike things. Every item description that connects a mundane object to cosmic significance practices intraphysics. The Dectus Medallion is half a piece of metal; it is also the key to the Altus Plateau; it is also a symbol of covenant; it is also a game mechanic. Finding the continuity between these—that is intraphysics.

cards.title: Machine of Absolute Rest
cards.description: 'By gyrostatic action, the Machine is transparent to successive intervals of time. It does not endure or continue to be, but rather conserves its contents outside of Time, sheltered from all phenomena.' Jarry's time machine isolates the body from duration—not accelerating through time but becoming still while time moves around you. The machine achieves travel not through motion but through absolute rest. Ranni's doll body is a machine of absolute rest. She has removed herself from the Erdtree's temporal order, neither enduring nor continuing to be in the normal sense. The doll doesn't age, doesn't decay, doesn't participate in the cycle of death and rebirth. It conserves her outside Time, sheltered from the phenomena of the Golden Order. To sit at a Site of Grace is to enter a local machine of absolute rest: time continues elsewhere while you are still.

cards.title: The Inconnu & Insolite
cards.description: 'The inconnu and the insolite await one at every step, littering one's path with things, events, and phenomena stranger than any surrealist invention.' The unknown and the unusual—not as exotic destinations but as constant companions. The world itself, properly attended to, produces endless strangeness without any need for artistic intervention. Surrealism tried to manufacture the marvelous; pataphysics notices it's already everywhere. The Lands Between literalizes this: every step reveals something stranger than invention could produce. A jar full of a person. A giant hand rising from the earth. A turtle wearing a pope hat. You don't have to seek the inconnu—it litters your path. The game understands that reality, when observed pataphysically, is already surreal. 'Trapped within our own limits, man, orphaned even by his hopes, holds fast to his neighbor, holds fast to his objects, holds fast to his memories.'

cards.title: Doctrine of Equivalence
cards.description: 'If a thing possesses a certain group of attributes, it must also possess the group of attributes contradictory to these.' This Hegelian principle becomes pataphysical doctrine: dialectics as equivalence, reality 'entirely constructed upon the principle of equivalence.' When Duchamp was praised by the Collège de 'Pataphysique, they noted that 'the approval of Marcel Duchamp was, for a pataphysician, infinitely greater than the Nobel Prize. Clearly, for an Optimate steeped in the Doctrine of Equivalence, this was not merely a figure of speech.' If Radagon possesses the attributes of devotion to the Golden Order, he must also possess the contradictory attributes. He does: he IS Marika, who shattered what he would restore. The Two Fingers speak wisdom AND babble meaninglessly. Every NPC who helps you also betrays you in some ending. The doctrine doesn't mean opposites cancel out—it means they coexist, equally real.

cards.title: Semantic Proliferation
cards.description: 'For the first time in Western culture one finds completely laid bare the aspect of a language that cannot stop itself, because it is never enclosed in a definitive statement; it announces its truth only in some future discourse entirely devoted to what it will have said. But even this future discourse itself does not have the power to halt the progression, and what it says is enclosed within it like a promise, a bequest to yet another discourse...' Foucault's diagnosis of 'the inability to stop the semantic proliferation once and for all in a single, master sentence.' The lore community is semantic proliferation institutionalized. VaatiVidya explains Miquella; someone counters; Vaati revises; new DLC arrives; the cycle continues. No master sentence can capture it. Each theory bequeaths itself to further theories. 'Language contains its own inner principle of proliferation'—and Miyazaki's fragmentary lore is designed to trigger it.

cards.title: Anoulipism & Synthoulipism
cards.description: The Oulipo—Ouvroir de Littérature Potentielle, 'Workshop of Potential Literature'—founded by François Le Lionnais and Raymond Queneau. Its dual practice: 'to research (anoulipism) and create (synthoulipism) forms and constraints with which literature has been or could be written.' Anoulipism discovers existing constraints; synthoulipism invents new ones. Perec's novel without the letter 'e' is synthoulipism. Finding that sonnets already constrain in particular ways is anoulipism. Miyazaki practices both. Anoulipism: discovering how Duchamp's Large Glass constrains, then working within those constraints. Synthoulipism: inventing the fragmentary lore system, the boss-locked narrative, the death-loop structure. The Oulipo recognized that all literature operates under constraints, visible or invisible—the pataphysician makes the constraints explicit and plays with them deliberately.

cards.title: Pataphysical Doppelgänger
cards.description: 'An entire city that becomes an alter ego or pataphysical doppelgänger to the modern metropolis. It is an entanglement of two worlds, where the excess in one world generates spatial bulges in the other.' The doppelgänger isn't a copy but an entanglement: two worlds connected such that what happens in one produces effects in the other. The Lands Between is the pataphysical doppelgänger of The Large Glass—not illustrating it but entangled with it, excess in Duchamp's work generating spatial bulges in Miyazaki's. The underground areas mirror the surface as doppelgänger, not copy. Mohg's Dynasty is the pataphysical doppelgänger of Leyndell—what Leyndell represses produces spatial bulges beneath it. 'Doting mother, intraphysics observes the games of her monsters.' The doppelgänger is the monster the original won't acknowledge.

cards.title: The Particular & The Irreducible
cards.description: 'Pataphysics is specifically the science of the particular and the irreducible.' Also: 'The irreducible is absurd. Let us reduce to absurdity to prove the evidence.' Not a contradiction but a method. General laws work by reducing particulars to instances of universals—this apple falls like all apples fall. Pataphysics refuses the reduction. This apple, here, now, falling in precisely this way, irreducible to any other apple. And since the irreducible cannot be explained by general laws, the irreducible is absurd from the perspective of science. But reduction to absurdity—showing that denying something leads to contradiction—proves the evidence by exhausting the alternatives. Every enemy in Elden Ring could be a generic 'soldier' or 'beast.' Instead: Godrick's soldiers, with their specific heraldry and specific dialogue. Irreducible. Absurd in their particularity. Proving something precisely by refusing to generalize it.

cards.title: Psychopathological Comedy
cards.description: 'The madman is comedy to the second degree, the deception of deception.' Dr. Faustroll is 'psychopathological comedy to the second degree, one who constantly unmasks the true folly of slavish devotion to rationalism and the unquestioning acceptance of scientific authority.' Comedy to the second degree: not just funny, but funny about what we consider funny. The deception of deception: not just fooling people, but revealing that the people who think they're wise are the real fools. Jarry 'comically destabilizes the notion that sanity and scientific reason are synonymous by undermining the certainty of scientific knowledge.' Patches is psychopathological comedy. He deceives you, but he also deceives the world's pretensions—the heroic quest, the noble purpose, the sane and rational order. The Dung Eater is the deception of deception: the game's logic of consumption and growth taken to its honest conclusion.

cards.title: Objective Chance
cards.description: 'Objective chance might also be considered a pataphysically sustained dialogue between desire (and/or the conscious or unconscious vagaries of a given individual), and the laws and exceptions, anomalies, and startling regularities governing worlds and planets.' Breton's surrealist concept, pataphysically extended. Not just coincidence, not just fate—a dialogue between what you want and what the universe provides. The meaningful accident. Finding the item you needed in the corpse of the enemy that killed you. The invasion that arrives at exactly the narrative moment it would mean the most. Objective chance is what speedrunners fight against and lore hunters cultivate. The pataphysician doesn't believe in coincidence OR determinism—they believe in dialogue, in call and response between consciousness and cosmos. Every 'lucky' drop is objective chance. Every 'random' encounter that feels fated.

cards.title: Quantum Pataphysics
cards.description: 'If physics proposes: "you have a brother and he likes cheese," then metaphysics replies: "If you have a brother, he likes cheese." But pataphysics says: "You don't have a brother and he likes cheese."' Georges Perec's syllogism describes quantum entanglement perfectly: particles that cannot be described independently even when separated. 'The quantum wave form instantaneously unrolls physics, metaphysics, and pataphysics in an infinite progression. Thus, we claim, quantum mechanics is pata-mechanics.' Einstein resisted: 'You believe in the God who plays dice, and I in complete law and order.' He was wrong. The universe is pataphysical. Schrödinger's cat—alive AND dead until observed—is pataphysical. 'The real is what became true when we opened the box... The imaginary is what did not become true.' Every quantum computation uses imaginary numbers—the square roots of negative numbers, which cannot exist in the real world. Pataphysics computed.

cards.title: Analogical Thought
cards.description: Breton writes that analogy in its surrealist meaning 'makes the mind apprehend the interdependence of two objects of thought located on different planes, between which thought's logical operations cannot cast any bridge and indeed oppose themselves a priori to any such act of bridging.' The ability to connect incompatible things through poetic rather than logical means. Surrealism's 'métascience du comme'—the meta-science of 'as' or 'like'—must continually seek and provoke new combinations across planes that logic declares unbridgeable. Every item description in Elden Ring practices analogical thought: a talisman IS a memory IS a power IS a piece of jewelry. The Erdtree IS a parasite IS grace IS imprisonment. Logic cannot bridge these planes; analogical thought leaps them. The discovery that Elden Ring corresponds to The Large Glass is itself an act of analogical thought—two objects on different planes, between which no logical bridge exists, suddenly seen as interdependent.

cards.title: The Exception
cards.description: 'What is a pataphysical gesture if not that which falls through reality to encounter itself on the other side of exception? It's no longer impossible because it has strangely happened; and yet it remains exceptional and so is not quite possible either. And yet it is here. We see it. Even if we don't quite believe what we see.' The exception occupies an impossible ontological position: it has happened, so it's not impossible; but it remains exceptional, so it's not quite possible either. It exists in the space between. The Tarnished is an exception—dead but not dead, chosen but rejected, heroic but hollow. Radagon IS Marika: impossible, yet it happened, yet we still don't quite believe what we see. Every demigod is an exception to the rules they supposedly embody. The game itself is an exception—a work of art that is also a commercial product that is also a spiritual practice. Pataphysics is 'the science of that which is superinduced upon metaphysics'—the laws governing exceptions.

cards.title: Sovereign Stupidity
