cards.description: 'When the hypothesis of intelligence ceases to be sovereign and becomes dominant, then it is the hypothesis of stupidity that becomes sovereign. A stupidity that might be said to be a sort of higher intelligence, on the verge of a radical thought—that is to say, beyond truth.' Baudrillard's concept, applied to Abramović's durational practice and, by extension, to video games. When intelligence becomes merely dominant—operational, efficient, productive—it loses its sovereignty. True sovereignty passes to stupidity: the refusal to optimize, the embrace of waste, the insistence on doing things the hard way. 'Radical unproductivity... counter-tactics to the operationalized speed of a culture of productivity—taking time and, in so doing, reclaiming time.' Miyazaki's games embody sovereign stupidity. No quest markers. No efficient paths. Deliberate waste of player time. A stupidity beyond truth—beyond the 'truth' that games should respect your time, that difficulty should be optional, that information should be clear. The sovereign stupid choice: git gud.

cards.title: The Neo-Scientific Novel
cards.description: The full title of Jarry's masterwork: 'Exploits and Opinions of Dr. Faustroll, Pataphysician (A Neo-Scientific Novel).' A genre that presents invented knowledge with the gravity of scientific fact—complete with precise measurements, technical vocabulary, and methodological rigor—while the content is patently absurd. The neo-scientific novel doesn't parody science; it practices science on impossible objects. Faustroll's measurements of God's surface area. The exact specifications of his sieve-boat. The chemical formula for his time machine. Elden Ring is a neo-scientific novel in game form. Item descriptions read like field notes. The game tracks exact damage numbers, scaling coefficients, frame data. The Golden Order has laws, theorems, fundamentals. All of this scientific apparatus applied to a world where a woman can become a tree, where death is a temporary inconvenience, where the moon is a person. Neo-scientific: rigorous method, impossible object.

cards.title: Definitional Literature
cards.description: A pataphysical writing practice theorized by the Oulipo: 'In a given statement, one replaces each significant word by one of its definitions from a given dictionary; the operation is repeated on the new sentence thus obtained, and so on.' Also called 'semo-definitional literature' or LSD (Littérature Sémo-Définitionnelle). Each word spirals into its definition, which spirals into further definitions, 'production automatique de littérature française.' The goal: revealing that language contains 'its own inner principle of proliferation'—meaning generates meaning generates meaning without end. The lore community practices definitional literature constantly. What is the Erdtree? A tree that is also grace that is also the Greater Will's instrument that is also a prison that is also... Each definition opens into further definitions. What is a Tarnished? The definition never stabilizes. 'One can operate definitional literature in the most mechanical way'—and the wiki does exactly that, mechanically expanding each term into its component definitions.

cards.title: Conscious Constraint
cards.description: The Oulipo's famous critique of surrealism: 'the absolute freedom of expression the latter seeks is no more than a slavery to laws—unconscious constraints—of which it is ignorant.' Automatic writing isn't free; it's enslaved to psychological, social, and linguistic constraints the writer doesn't recognize. 'One purpose of the Oulipian formal constraint will be to free artistic imagination and creation from such unconscious constraints, replacing them with paradoxically liberating and conscious constraint.' Freedom through chosen limitation. The sonnet liberates by constraining. The lipogram (writing without a letter) liberates by forbidding. Miyazaki's design philosophy is conscious constraint. The stamina bar constrains—and liberates by forcing tactical thinking. The lack of pause constrains—and liberates by making every moment consequential. The fragmentary lore constrains—and liberates the player's imagination. Surrealism's 'freedom' produces cliché; conscious constraint produces genuine novelty.

cards.title: The Celibate Machine
cards.description: Michel Carrouges identified 'celibate machines' across the works of 'Jarry, Roussel, Duchamp, and Kafka'—mechanical systems that operate in isolation, producing nothing useful, serving no reproductive or productive function. Duchamp's 'Bachelor Machine' in The Large Glass: the Bachelors grind chocolate, produce illuminating gas, but never reach the Bride. The machine runs forever without consummation. Deleuze and Guattari discuss celibate machines in Anti-Oedipus as desire-production that refuses to produce anything social. 'Duchamp's bachelor machine, Étant donnés, turns the gaze back on the gaze.' Every Divine Tower is a celibate machine—elaborate apparatus producing nothing but the restoration of runes you already earned. The Roundtable Hold: a machine for producing quests that never resolve. The Golden Order itself: a celibate machine grinding grace, producing only more grinding. Anti-Bachelor Machines would be those that break the cycle—but do any of Elden Ring's endings actually escape the machine?

cards.title: Absolute Love
cards.description: Jarry's 1899 novel exploring the extreme mental states of devotion. 'Jarry's accounts of extreme mental states in Absolute Love are based upon his extensive readings of the same French psychologists, such as Pierre Janet and Jean-Martin Charcot, who significantly influenced Freud.' Love pushed past reason into pathology—not romantic love but absolute love, love without object, love as pure intensity. The novel 'humorously erased the lines between the conscious and unconscious, reality and dream, the repressed and expressed.' Miquella inspires absolute love—Mohg's obsession, the Haligtree pilgrims' devotion, the player's compulsion to save him. Ranni inspires it. Melina perhaps. These aren't characters you like; they're characters who induce extreme mental states. The game itself induces absolute love in its players: devotion beyond reason, hundreds of hours given to a world that offers nothing back. Jarry understood that love, pushed far enough, becomes indistinguishable from madness—and both become indistinguishable from religious experience.

cards.title: Coition Through the Glass Pane
cards.description: Duchamp wrote that 'the penalty consists in cutting through the glass pane'—echoing Jarry's lovers who make 'love through the wall.' The glass as transparent barrier where intimate acts occur but never consummate. In The Large Glass, the Bride and Bachelors desire each other eternally but never touch—separated by the glass itself, the material of the artwork. The shop window: you see what you want, you press against it, but the glass remains. William Anastasi argues this is why Duchamp's glass works all 'accidentally' broke—Jarry's three novels all climax with sexual union accompanied by shattering glass. The screen is our glass pane. We spend hundreds of hours in intimate relation with the Lands Between, touching it through controllers, but the glass remains. Every Site of Grace reaches toward us; we reach back. The covenant never consummates. We make love through the wall, and the wall is what makes the love possible.

cards.title: L'Accident
cards.description: The Large Glass 'accidentally' shattered in transit in 1926. Duchamp declared he loved the cracks—they completed the work. He meticulously reproduced every shard in Box in a Valise. But William Anastasi notes: all three of Duchamp's major glass works broke. All three of Jarry's novels climax with sexual union accompanied by shattering glass. Pataphysics studies 'purely accidental phenomena'—but in pataphysics, there is no such thing as pure accident. The intentional accident: designing chance, willing the unwilled, making the accident happen on purpose while maintaining its accidental nature. Duchamp signed letters 'Yours Pataphysically.' The cracks weren't damage; they were consummation. In games, bugs become features. Speedrunners exploit 'accidents' that were always latent in the code. The discovery that Elden Ring corresponds to The Large Glass—was it accident or intention? Both. Neither. L'Accident: the pataphysical event that was always going to happen, that couldn't have happened, that happened anyway.

cards.title: The Androgyne
cards.description: Jarry's characters cross-dress; his narratives blur gender until it dissolves. Duchamp became Rrose Sélavy—not a disguise but an alternate self, photographed by Man Ray, signing artworks, existing as fully as Marcel. Both artists understood: the division of one into two (male/female, artist/artwork, player/game) is artificial. The androgyne reunites what was never truly separate. Radagon IS Marika. Not 'was once' or 'became' or 'merged with'—IS. The Golden Order's deepest secret is that its god is an androgyne, two-who-are-one, the unity the Order itself forbids. Marika shatters what Radagon would restore; Radagon builds what Marika would break. They are the same hand working against itself. The Doctrine of Equivalence made flesh: if a thing possesses certain attributes, it must also possess the contradictory attributes. The androgyne doesn't reconcile opposites—it reveals they were never opposed. Rrose Sélavy. Radagon Marika. The Bride who is also, somehow, a Bachelor.

cards.title: Caesura
cards.description: The break in the line. In poetry, the pause where you breathe, where rhythm suspends. In swordplay—as Patrick Rothfuss names Kvothe's blade in The Kingkiller Chronicle—the break that ends the dance, the final cut. The poetic break becomes the literal break; the metaphor becomes the blade. Michel de Certeau theorized tactical reversals, gaps in dominant systems where resistance slips through—and his name sounds like the concept he theorizes. De Certeau / Caesura. The name as sympathetic magic: he writes about breaks because he IS a break. Rothfuss's naming magic works the same way—the true name links to the thing's nature. In Elden Ring: the Shattering was a caesura in the Golden Order's verse. Each boss death is a caesura—the break that ends the combat poem. Sites of Grace are caesuras—pauses where you breathe before the next line. The game is written in breaks. The lore lives in the gaps. The meaning rushes into the pause.

cards.title: Pataquericals
cards.description: 'Markers of stigmatized difference.' Things that don't fit, that get noticed, that fall outside. Like language in poetry—called to notice when it deviates from ordinary use. Pataquericals are negatives, but 'negatives have a queer way of becoming positives, especially for those for whom such tactical reversals might create a space of greater freedom' (de Certeau). Reverse pataquericals: 'innovator, rebel, extraordinary, exceptional, iconoclast, genius... special.' To take on the stigma as badge of honor, 'if not with pride then with defiance.' The Tarnished is a pataqueerical—maidenless, graceless, stigmatized, yet through tactical reversal becoming Elden Lord. The player is pataqueerical—wasting hundreds of hours on a 'mere game,' stigmatized as escapist, yet finding something the productive world cannot offer. 'Stumpling clumpsiness for one, the eloquence of the gods for 'nother.' The same action read two ways depending on who's reading.

cards.title: The Anti-Bachelor Machine
cards.description: The bachelor machine: 'nonproductive, non-procreative, onanistic processes, vicious (or self-enclosing/collapsing) circles, an apparatus that is unable to get outside itself.' Duchamp's Bachelors grinding chocolate forever, never reaching the Bride. Kafka's Penal Colony inscribing punishment endlessly. But what breaks the circle? The anti-bachelor machine. Connected to délire—'that which goes astray, deviates from the rational, errs, raves.' The anti-bachelor machine doesn't produce efficiently; it produces escape. It raves its way out of the vicious circle. Which Elden Ring endings are anti-bachelor machines? The Golden Order endings just restart the cycle—bachelor machines grinding grace. But Ranni's ending? The Frenzied Flame? These might be anti-bachelor machines—apparatuses that finally get outside themselves, even if 'outside' means annihilation or exile. Délire as liberation: going astray as the only way to go somewhere new.

cards.title: The Extraordinary Ordinary
cards.description: 'The extraordinary is never more than an extension of the ordinary.' Not a separate category but a continuation—the ordinary pushed further, taken seriously, followed to its conclusion. Every demigod is an extension of mortal ambition. Every cosmic horror is an extension of ordinary fear. The Golden Order is an extension of the ordinary desire for meaning. The pataphysical insight: there is no transcendence, only extension. The Tarnished doesn't become something other than human—they extend humanity into spaces it doesn't usually go. The game doesn't offer escape from the ordinary—it extends the ordinary into the extraordinary and shows they were always the same line. This is why the lore works: it's not fantasy disconnected from reality, it's reality extended until it becomes unrecognizable while remaining, somehow, familiar. The extraordinary ordinary: the world you know, pushed past the point where you recognize it, still the same world.

cards.title: Frame Analysis
cards.description: Erving Goffman's 'counterintuitive idea is that an "event" (including an art "object") does not speak for itself but is recognizable only by its frame or context. For this reason, the discussion about an event can exceed the duration of the "event" itself. An event, or work of art, like a dream, may elicit multiple—incommensurable or discrepant—frames. Some frames are sticky, become stigma.' Furthermore: 'what is out-of-frame is often (in the end) most significant: what is is defined by what it isn't.' Related to Wittgenstein's 'seeing as'—the fundamental nature of perception through frames. Every item description in Elden Ring is a frame for the object. The wiki is a frame. The lore video is a frame. VaatiVidya's interpretation frames Miquella differently than Zullie's. Some frames become sticky—become the 'official' reading. But what's out-of-frame is most significant: what the description doesn't say defines the object as much as what it does. The discovery that Elden Ring corresponds to The Large Glass is a reframing—suddenly everything looks different through a new lens.

cards.title: Nepohumanism
cards.description: 'Nepohumanism (also called necrohumanism and hypohumanism) universalizes one's immediate preferences while stigmatizing as barbaric those that are not immediately intelligible.' The move that declares your local values to be universal truths, and everything outside them to be heresy, madness, or evil. The Golden Order is nepohumanist—it universalizes the Greater Will's preferences while stigmatizing as barbaric (Omen, Those Who Live in Death, dragon communion, frenzied flame) everything not immediately intelligible to its framework. The Two Fingers speak universal law; what they cannot interpret is declared monstrous. Queen Marika's order: 'My Order... the Order.' Not 'an order'—THE order. Nepohumanism is the disease the Golden Order spreads: the inability to recognize that your frame is a frame, not reality itself. Every fundamentalism is nepohumanist. Every 'common sense' that declares alternatives insane.

cards.title: Pataqueronormativities
cards.description: 'I just want to be like everyone else like me.' The paradox of wanting to fit in with the misfits, to be normal among the abnormal, to find your people and then police the boundaries. 'But aesthetics is not a tool of morality, nor is it bound to it or by it. Aesthetics is not applied ethics.' And: 'It is practically an Ironclad law of nature that new orthodoxies replace old ones, just as it is a delusion of starry-eyed romanticism to think anything else is possible. Yet think we must for a growing imaginary.' The Roundtable Hold is pataqueronormative—a gathering of exceptions who immediately establish norms for how exceptions should behave. The lore community is pataqueronormative—outsiders who create insider knowledge, stigmatized players who stigmatize casual players. Every counterculture becomes a culture. Every revolution becomes an institution. The pataqueerical resists even its own normativities.

cards.title: Animalady
cards.description: 'Beyond the fear of ethnic or racial difference is the fear of animal difference. The fear of animalady manifests as the need to pass as human (cultivated, learned, well mannered, well dressed), which is figured, in the tale of Dr. Tarr and Professor Fether, as the need to pass as not insane.' The deepest stigma: not passing as human at all. Michael McClure 'directly confronts the phobia of not passing as human' by performing 'Ghost Tantra' to growling lions, 'a full dissolve of the human into animality... a marvelously incantatory, almost satiric exorcism of the human.' The Omen are animalady made flesh—horns, tails, monstrous features that mark them as failing to pass as human. The grafted. The beastmen. Those Who Live in Death. The Golden Order's deepest fear is animalady: that beneath the cultivated human there is something that doesn't pass, something animal, something mad. Rykard feeding himself to the serpent: embracing animalady as liberation. 'He wants to pass as animal.'

cards.title: Passing
cards.description: 'American culture is filled with both the desire to pass and a resistance to passing: to be absorbed by the dominant culture or to remake that culture. Assimilation is motivated as much by fear as by desire.' To pass: to be read as belonging when you don't, to hide your difference, to move through spaces that would exclude you if they knew. Larry Eigner: 'After trying my animal noise i break out with a man's cry.' The Tarnished passes—moves through the Lands Between as if they belong, when they're actually the walking dead, the graceless made graceful again by arbitrary gift. Rogier passes until his corruption shows. Fia passes as a simple deathbed companion. Everyone in the Roundtable is passing as something they're not. But there's also resistance to passing: Rykard refuses to pass as human. The Dung Eater refuses to pass as sane. Some misfits don't want absorption—they want to remake the culture entirely. 'It is class consciousness, not nose jobs, that erases difference.'

cards.title: Otherworldly Humanity
cards.description: George Kuchar's phrase for the pataqueerical quest. In Thundercrack!, Kuchar 'plays a circus truck driver who has fallen in love with the female gorilla in his charge. In the final, touching scene we see the driver in bed with someone in a very campy gorilla suit.' This is 'the perfect realization of the possibility of the pataque(e)rical as a quest for otherworldly humanity.' 'With its parodic bestiality, Kuchar reverses the normal direction of assimilation: he wants to pass as animal.' Otherworldly humanity: not transcending the human but finding the human in what's declared inhuman, finding kinship with the monstrous, loving across the boundary of species/sanity/category. Ranni's questline is a quest for otherworldly humanity—the player falling in love with a doll inhabited by a ghost who murdered her own body. Miquella's followers seek otherworldly humanity in their sleeping god. The pataqueerical doesn't want to be more human; it wants to find humanity in the other world.

cards.title: The Last Refuge
cards.description: 'Moral repugnance is the last refuge of scoundrels.' When you can't argue against something on its merits, declare it disgusting. When you can't engage with art intellectually, call it offensive. The Golden Order's response to everything outside its frame: not 'this is wrong' but 'this is disgusting, aberrant, unclean.' The Omen aren't argued against—they're treated as inherently repugnant. The Dung Eater isn't debated—he's declared monstrous. This is the nepohumanist move: moral repugnance as substitute for engagement. Fox News attacked Thundercrack! as 'disgusting' because engagement would require acknowledging what it was actually doing. The Greater Will doesn't argue with the Frenzied Flame—it simply declares it abomination. But moral repugnance reveals more about the repulsed than the repulsive. The scoundrel hides behind disgust because they have nothing else.

cards.title: Merdre!
cards.description: 'The (in)famous neological euphemism that is the first word spoken in Jarry's Ubu Roi: "Merdre!"' Shit with an extra letter. The clinamen applied to profanity—a tiny swerve that transforms the expected into the unexpected. When Ubu spoke this word on opening night in 1896, it caused a riot. The audience heard 'merde' (shit) but saw 'merdre'—same but different, obscene but not quite, the familiar made strange by a single letter. This is pataphysics in miniature: the smallest possible deviation creating the maximum possible disruption. The 'r' is a clinamen. It swerves the word from its expected path. Atoms are to matter what letters are to words—and one letter changes everything. Every FromSoftware neologism works this way: 'Tarnished' (tarnish + vanished?), 'Elden' (elder + golden?), 'Erdtree' (earth + tree but German). The clinamen at the level of the letter. Merdre!

cards.title: The Two Laws
cards.description: 'Pataphysics is strictly regulated by two laws: those of the syzygy and those of the clinamen, both of which pertain to the broad relationship of matter to motion.' Syzygy: conjunction without loss of identity. Clinamen: the swerve that escapes determinism. Compare to the Golden Order's Two Laws: Regression (all things converge to their source) and Causality (cause precedes effect, determinism enforced). The parallel is exact—and exactly inverted. Where Syzygy allows conjunction while preserving difference, Regression demands convergence that erases difference. Where Clinamen liberates through the tiny swerve, Causality binds through iron necessity. The Golden Order's laws are the nepohumanist perversion of pataphysical laws: what should liberate is made to constrain, what should preserve identity is made to erase it. Radagon/Marika embodies true syzygy: two beings conjoined who RETAIN their opposing identities—Marika shatters, Radagon repairs, neither absorbed into the other. They orbit the Elden Ring in eternal opposition, locked in astronomical syzygy. The Golden Order's Law of Regression would demand they merge into one will; instead they remain two, trapped in conjunction. The pataphysician knows both pairs of laws—and knows which set leads to freedom.

cards.title: The Cosmological Syzygy
cards.description: As above, so below. The TYCHOS model proposes Sun and Mars as binary partners orbiting each other, with Earth at the barycenter. The Lands Between mirrors this structure: Radagon/Marika as binary pair, the Erdtree as central axis, and Radahn as the gravitational force holding it all in place. Radahn learned gravity magic to freeze the celestial order—to hold the stars, to stop fate from progressing. Two bindings maintain the frozen world: the Elden Ring (terrestrial) and Radahn's gravity (celestial). Marika shatters the first; the Tarnished must break the second. Only when Radahn falls do the stars move again—only then can new orders emerge. Ranni's path opens. Miquella's ambition becomes possible. In the DLC, Miquella doesn't just defeat the celestial holder; he incorporates Radahn, charms him, makes him consort. But this is false syzygy—one will dominating another, regression disguised as conjunction. True syzygy preserves both identities in opposition. Miquella's order fails because he cannot allow opposition; he must charm, must absorb, must regress.

cards.title: Colleges of Unreason
cards.description: Fictional institutions in Samuel Butler's Erewhon that train students in hypothetics - the study of impossible contingencies and unreasonable scenarios. These colleges complement reason with unreason, teaching that extreme rationality unchecked can lead to irrationality, and that creative inquiry requires both.

cards.title: Hypothetics
cards.description: The study of imaginary and impossible contingencies in Butler's Colleges of Unreason. A curriculum focusing on a poetics of potentiality and unrealized states of possible realities, preparing students for the unexpected through speculation rather than certainty.

cards.title: UnderAcademy College
cards.description: An unaccredited pataphysical institution founded in 2011 by Talan Memmott offering anti-degree programs. Students receive certificates as Calidus Parvulus, Paratus Adversario, or Caput Magnum, prioritizing knowledge acquisition over qualification through online classes emphasizing critical play and production.

cards.title: Digressors
cards.description: The faculty at UnderAcademy College, termed in opposition to 'professors.' Artists and writers interested in exploring methods of dissemination and instruction while learning through interaction with participants.

cards.title: Anti-Degree
cards.description: Pataphysical academic credentials offered by UnderAcademy College that emphasize knowledge production rather than professional qualification. Three levels: Calidus Parvulus (crafty little one), Paratus Adversario (prepared adversary), and Caput Magnum (big head).

cards.title: Neoliberal University
cards.description: Bill Reading's concept of universities transformed from ideological arms of the nation-state into autonomous bureaucratic corporations driven by market competition and the commodification of research and teaching. Characterized by dialogues of 'excellence' that streamline all activities under managerial infrastructure.

cards.title: Ethos of the Unknown
cards.description: Alan Liu's concept describing how participants inside contemporary knowledge systems can paradoxically appear to stand outside them. Applied to patacademies, it suggests dispersed knowledge economies beyond the traditional academy's jurisdiction.

cards.title: Speculative Learning
cards.description: Learning focused on conditional knowledge and possibilities rather than objective facts. In pataphysics, described as 'learned and inutilious research' that complements traditional scholarship while resisting commodification of knowledge.

cards.title: Patacademy
cards.description: Parodic or alternative academic institutions based on pataphysical principles, such as the Collège de 'Pataphysique or UnderAcademy College. These institutions supplement rather than replace traditional education, emphasizing ludic, performative, and imaginative activities.

cards.title: Culture Jamming
cards.description: Subversive tactics employed in pataphysical pedagogy, such as using memes instead of traditional essays or reverse-engineering syllabi. A method of short-circuiting bureaucratic apparatuses and discourses of excellence in traditional universities.

cards.title: Enactive Knowledge
cards.description: Knowledge acquired through performative learning and critical experiential engagement rather than passive absorption. Butler advocated learning by doing rather than rehearsing: 'let your falls be bona fide falls in the rough and tumble of the world.'

cards.title: Melbourne Institute of Pataphysical Studies
cards.description: One of several contemporary pataphysical institutes emphasizing performance, play, workshops, creative performance, multicultural activism, and ludic critical activity in their cultural mission.

cards.title: Académie Québécoise de 'Pataphysique
cards.description: Quebec-based pataphysical academy that emphasizes performance and play through workshops and creative activities, enunciating the partiality of knowledge over notions of objective truths.

cards.title: London Institute of 'Pataphysics
cards.description: British pataphysical organization connected to Atlas Press that maintains pataphysical research and publishing activities. Part of the network of international pataphysical institutes.

cards.title: SpecLab
cards.description: The Speculative Computing Laboratory at University of Virginia directed by Johanna Drucker. Pushed subjective and probabilistic concepts of knowledge as experience (partial, situated, subjective) against objective mechanistic claims for knowledge as information.

cards.title: Calidus Parvulus
cards.description: Latin for 'crafty little one.' The first level anti-degree at UnderAcademy College, equivalent to a bachelor's degree, completed with four courses. Emphasizes cleverness and adaptability.

cards.title: Paratus Adversario
cards.description: Latin for 'prepared adversary.' The second level anti-degree at UnderAcademy College, equivalent to a master's degree, requiring an additional class and thesis project beyond Calidus Parvulus.

cards.title: Caput Magnum
cards.description: Latin for 'big head.' The highest level anti-degree at UnderAcademy College, equivalent to a doctorate. The terminal credential in the pataphysical anti-degree hierarchy.

cards.title: Partial Knowledge
cards.description: Situated and conditional knowledge that acknowledges its incompleteness and perspective. Contrasted with claims to objective or total knowledge. Central to speculative computing and pataphysical pedagogy.

cards.title: Hall of Uselessness
cards.description: Simon Leys's concept of an ideal university that would deliver no degrees or professional qualifications, motivated solely by students' personal desire for knowledge. Knowledge acquisition would be its only reward.

cards.title: Useful Uselessness
cards.description: The paradox of pataphysical education: knowledge pursued without utilitarian aims that nonetheless produces valuable critical and creative capacities. Questions whether education must necessarily imply utility.

cards.title: Quantum Computing
cards.description: Computing based on quantum mechanical phenomena including superposition and entanglement. In pataphysics, represents a computational approach to exceptions and contradictions through qubits that exist in multiple states simultaneously.

cards.title: Qubit
cards.description: A quantum bit that can exist in superposition of both 0 and 1 states simultaneously. The fundamental unit of quantum computing, embodying pataphysical principles of contradiction and multiple simultaneous truths.

cards.title: Quantum Entanglement
cards.description: Quantum phenomenon where particles become correlated such that the state of one instantaneously affects the state of another regardless of distance. Applied to pataphysics as a model for understanding syzygy and remote connections.

cards.title: Superposition
cards.description: The quantum principle that a system can exist in multiple states simultaneously until measured. Pataphysically significant as a computational model for holding contradictions and exceptions together.

cards.title: Uboolean Logic
cards.description: A pataphysical alternative to Boolean logic that includes FalsTrue as a third value alongside True and False. Named after Père Ubu, it allows for contradictory states and exceptions in computational systems.

cards.title: FalsTrue
cards.description: The third truth value in Uboolean logic, representing statements that are simultaneously false and true. A computational embodiment of pataphysical contradiction and the logic of exceptions.

cards.title: PRASCAL
cards.description: A pataphysical programming language incorporating Uboolean logic with FalsTrue values. Enables computation with contradictions and exceptions, moving beyond binary true/false distinctions.

cards.title: Syzygy Surfer
cards.description: A pataphysical search tool designed to find connections through syzygy, clinamen, and anomaly rather than conventional Boolean search. Seeks unexpected alignments and coincidences across datasets.

cards.title: Patalgorithm
cards.description: Algorithms designed according to pataphysical principles, incorporating exceptions, contradictions, and swerves rather than deterministic logic. Emphasize anomaly search and unexpected connections.

cards.title: Patadata
cards.description: Data structured according to pataphysical principles, emphasizing the exceptional, the anomalous, and the contradictory rather than the typical or average. Contrasts with conventional metadata focused on standardization.

cards.title: Panalogy
cards.description: Jorge Luis Borges's concept where every object can be analogized to every other object. In pataphysical computing, supports finding unexpected connections across disparate domains through universal analogical possibility.

cards.title: Anomaly Search
cards.description: Search methodology focused on finding exceptions, outliers, and anomalies rather than typical cases. Central to pataphysical approaches to data analysis and information retrieval.

cards.title: Semantic Web
cards.description: Tim Berners-Lee's vision of a web of data with machine-readable metadata describing relationships between resources. Pataphysically relevant for enabling unexpected connections through ontologies and RDF triples.

cards.title: Ontology (Computing)
cards.description: In computing, a formal representation of knowledge defining classes of objects and relationships between them. Pataphysical ontologies emphasize exceptions, contradictions, and alternative classifications.

cards.title: RDF (Resource Description Framework)
cards.description: A framework for representing information using subject-predicate-object triples. Enables semantic web connections and pataphysical discovery of unexpected relationships between resources.

cards.title: Metacrap
cards.description: Cory Doctorow's term for the problems with metadata: people lie, cannot agree on categorization, are lazy, and have differing incentives. Relevant to pataphysical critique of attempts to standardize and totalize knowledge.

cards.title: Clinamen Search
cards.description: Search methodology based on the atomic swerve, looking for deviations and unexpected angles rather than direct matches. Finds knowledge through deviation and tangent rather than straight lines.

cards.title: Deep Learning
cards.description: Machine learning using neural networks with multiple layers. Pataphysically significant for its ability to find unexpected patterns and connections in data that defy human logical categorization.

cards.title: Disambiguation
cards.description: The process of resolving ambiguity to determine correct meaning. Pataphysics often embraces rather than resolves ambiguity, seeing multiple meanings as valuable rather than problematic.

cards.title: The Pataphysical Studios
cards.description: An early digital pataphysical project that created a Pataphysical Slot Machine and explored time machine concepts computationally, pioneering applications of pataphysical principles to digital media.

cards.title: ALAMO
cards.description: Atelier de littérature assistée par la mathématique et les ordinateurs - Workshop for literature assisted by mathematics and computers. An Oulipian working group exploring computational approaches to constrained writing.

cards.title: ARTA
cards.description: Atelier de Recherches et Techniques Avancées - Workshop for Advanced Research and Techniques. An Oulipian group exploring advanced computational and mathematical methods for literary creation.

cards.title: TEAnO
cards.description: Telematica, Elettronica, Analisi nell'Opificio - Telematics, Electronics, Analysis in the Workshop. An Italian Oulipian working group focused on electronic and computational literary techniques.

cards.title: Combinatorialism
cards.description: The Oulipian practice of systematically exploring combinations of literary elements. Raymond Queneau's Cent mille milliards de poèmes exemplifies this through combinatorial poetry generating trillions of possible sonnets.

cards.title: Algorhythm
cards.description: A portmanteau of algorithm and rhythm, suggesting computational procedures that incorporate musical or temporal patterns. Relevant to pataphysical approaches combining logic with aesthetic and temporal dimensions.

cards.title: Constraint-Based Writing
cards.description: The Oulipian practice of writing under arbitrary constraints (e.g., lipograms, palindromes) to generate creativity through limitation. Constraints become generative rather than restrictive.

cards.title: Cent Mille Milliards de Poèmes
cards.description: Raymond Queneau's 1961 combinatorial poetry book of ten sonnets where each line can be combined with any line in the same position from other sonnets, generating 10^14 possible poems.

cards.title: Nicolas Bourbaki
cards.description: The collective pseudonym of a group of mathematicians who wrote rigorous foundational texts. Influenced the Oulipo's approach to mathematical rigor in literary constraints and structural analysis.

cards.title: Ivanhoe Game
cards.description: A digital role-playing game developed by Jerome McGann and Johanna Drucker at SpecLab. Players intervene in literary texts through creative critical moves, embodying patacritical principles of subjective interpretation and transformative engagement with texts.

cards.title: Comparative Vandalism
cards.description: A concept developed by artist Asger Jorn as an alternative to academic art history, emphasizing Nordic folk art and archeology. The Scandinavian Institute of Comparative Vandalism studied cultural transformation through acts of alteration and reclamation.

cards.title: L.H.O.O.Q.
cards.description: Duchamp's famous readymade featuring the Mona Lisa with an upturned mustache and goatee. When read aloud in French, it translates as 'elle a chaud au cul' (she has a hot ass), demonstrating irreverent attitude toward canonical art traditions.

cards.title: Modifications
cards.description: A series of paintings by Asger Jorn created by painting over preexisting oil paintings found in antique shops and flea markets. Amateur works chosen for their sentimental or kitsch value were transformed by Jorn's vibrant overpainting.

cards.title: Détournement
cards.description: A Situationist method of changing or modifying the context or nature of a presently-existing cultural object so that it acquires a new and higher significance. Used by artists like Jorn to transform found paintings and cultural artifacts.

cards.title: Combine Print
cards.description: A term invented by artist James Brewton to describe artworks utilizing multiple printing techniques, including offset lithography, screenprinting, and hand-made stamps, all combined in a single work.

cards.title: Graffiti Pataphysic
cards.description: Title of James Brewton's 1965 exhibition at Kenmore Galleries, Philadelphia, drawing on Jarry's pataphysics and the raw, untutored expression of street graffiti. Brewton saw graffiti as available for all humankind, unlike modern art reserved for the privileged few.

cards.title: Evergreen Review
cards.description: American literary magazine whose May-June 1960 issue 'What Is Pataphysics?' became one of the most important English-language publications devoted to Alfred Jarry's life and work, introducing pataphysics to American audiences.

cards.title: Only the Comic is Serious
cards.description: Famous line from Roger Shattuck's introduction to pataphysics in Evergreen Review, frequently deployed by artists like James Brewton in statements and catalogs. Captures the pataphysical principle that humor is the vehicle for profound truth.

cards.title: Neoscientific Novel
cards.description: Jarry's own designation for Exploits and Opinions of Dr. Faustroll, Pataphysician, presenting a highly imaginative alternative universe where absurd and contradictory propositions make sense, anticipating quantum physics and twentieth-century scientific developments.

cards.title: The Pataphysics Times
cards.description: A cyanotype newspaper-broadsheet created by James Brewton in 1964, permanently displayed in gallery windows. A labyrinthine collage serving as a blueprint for artistic revolution in Philadelphia based on Jarry's science of imaginary solutions.

cards.title: Cyanotype
cards.description: A photographic printing process producing cyan-blue prints, commonly used by engineers and architects for blueprints. James Brewton used this process for his pataphysical newspaper The Pataphysics Times.

cards.title: Hors Concors
cards.description: French phrase meaning 'out of competition,' referencing Duchamp's Fountain being rejected from the 1917 Society of Independent Artists exhibition. Used in pataphysical artworks to mark works excluded from official recognition.

cards.title: Linguatilt
cards.description: A term suggestive of the lettristic clinamen—the swerve or deviation in language. Used to describe linguistic transformations and shifts in meaning that occur through playful manipulation of words and letters.

cards.title: Ordure
cards.description: French word for excrement or filth. James Brewton punned on this in his translation of Jacques Prévert's weather forecast from 'Urdu,' embracing the scatological aspect of Jarry's writings and those who followed in his footsteps.

cards.title: American Dream-Girl
cards.description: Title of James Brewton's 1965 exhibition at Galerie AP in Copenhagen, referencing his obsession with Jarry's pataphysics and the 'American Dream-Girl' of adult magazines and popular culture.

cards.title: Ineluctable Logic
cards.description: The unavoidable or inescapable logic of pataphysics, as described by James Brewton. Refers to the internal consistency of Jarry's absurdist reasoning, such as the famous tripod with four legs that nonetheless makes perfect pataphysical sense.

cards.title: Patatarget
cards.description: A tondo painting by James Brewton featuring an esoteric, quasi-scientific vocabulary of imagery. The work reveals the creative excitement of applying Jarry's tongue-in-cheek philosophy of pataphysics to visual art.

cards.title: Patacomic
cards.description: A comic planned by James Brewton based on Jarry's Le Surmâle (The Supermale), scheduled to launch on April 1, 1965. Its nonappearance suggests it was designed as an April Fools' Day joke, itself a pataphysical gesture.

cards.title: Suicide by Hallucination
cards.description: Title of Roger Shattuck's chapter on Jarry in The Banquet Years, depicting Jarry's short life as a slow 'suicide by hallucination' through luminiferous ether and alcohol. Represents literary martyrdom through self-destructive commitment to art.

cards.title: Collège de 'Pataphysique
cards.description: Official organization founded to preserve and extend Jarry's pataphysical philosophy. Numerous artists including Asger Jorn and James Brewton were inspired by its official-sounding societies and institutes to create their own pataphysical institutions.

cards.title: Menippean Satire
cards.description: A tradition of comic satire characterized by stylistic heterogeneity, logical inversions, and carnivalesque humor. Not a single integral text but a patchwork belonging to various genres, prose and poetry, with multiple styles and registers.

cards.title: Gunslinger
cards.description: Edward Dorn's narco-transgenic, serial mock epic of the postatomic American West (1968-1975). A comic poem incorporating pataphysical elements including clinamina, dead letters, ontological anomalies, and puns, following Jarry's influence.

cards.title: Syzygy
cards.description: One of two laws regulating pataphysics. In astronomy, a conjunction or opposition of two planets in orbit around a third. In biology, conjunction of two organisms without loss of identity. In language, momentary oppositions and conjunctions in verbal meanings (puns and portmanteaux).

cards.title: Clinamen
cards.description: From ancient Greek particle physics via Lucretius: the swerve or deviation of an atom from its path, causing collision with other atoms to produce new formations. In pataphysics and poetry, the clinamen marks deviation from stable flow, the moment where law is insufficient to prevent aberration.

cards.title: Ethernity
cards.description: Jarry's portmanteau word fusing ether and eternity, anticipating Einstein's fusion of space and time. Describes immobile ether that is not luminiferous, contrasting with perishable, circularly mobile luminiferous ether.

cards.title: Nightletta
cards.description: In Edward Dorn's Gunslinger, a letter brought by a goggled deliverer dangling from a biplane, signed by the deceased character 'i'. Exemplifies pataphysical communication between the dead and the living.

cards.title: Correlations of Expectations
cards.description: Jarry's argument that laws are not rules but merely correlations of expectations in relation to which the anomaly is the repressed part ensuring the rule doesn't work. Laws govern the general; pataphysics studies the particular.

cards.title: Marvelous Accidentalism
cards.description: Term from Edward Dorn's Gunslinger describing narrative fueled by skewed causality where reference is uncertain and representation subject to abrupt shifts of subtext. Related to genetic mutation and the clinamen.

cards.title: Palindromic Ontology
cards.description: A species of canceling reversibility where things read the same forwards and backwards, establishing linguistic and existential symmetry. In Gunslinger: 'since I is the same back as ahead,' exemplifying pataphysical reversibility.

cards.title: Literate Projector
cards.description: A pataphysical device in Gunslinger that reverses cinema's material trajectory: when a 35mm strip is put through it, it turns it into a script instantaneously, projecting the finished script onto the screen. It will 'Invent a whole new literatūre which was Already There.'

cards.title: Durée
cards.description: Henri Bergson's concept of duration: a condition of mental and material fluidity abolishing causality and determination. When restored to original purity, appears as wholly qualitative multiplicity, an absolute heterogeneity of elements passing over into each other.

cards.title: Timetrain
cards.description: The vehicle of the Slinger's departure in Gunslinger, traveling faster than both the speed of light and prime numbers. Represents pataphysical transcendence of conventional temporal and spatial constraints.

cards.title: bpNichol
cards.description: Late Canadian poet, cofounder of the Canadian College of 'Pataphysics (1979). Creator of the 'Pataphysical Hardware Company and author of Probable Systems—instantiations of pataphysical cognition exploring lettristic patalogic.

cards.title: 'Pataphysical Hardware Company
cards.description: Founded by bpNichol in 1944 (year of his birth), manifesting in May 1985 in Toronto. Offered 'everything for your imaginary needs' through catalog of items including Thought Balloons, double-ended pencils, and 'Plaster de Paris.'

cards.title: Notation
cards.description: For bpNichol, the conscious act of noting things down for the voice, instructing the eye on the movement of the tongue for the pleasure of the ear. Fusion of eye, ear, and mouth in thoroughly material poetics.

cards.title: No Tation
cards.description: bpNichol's pataphysical demonstration where the word 'notation' erases itself: 'There is no tation.' Something that exists even as you know it doesn't exist, leading through a chain of pataphysical logic where each step coheres only for the length of transition.

cards.title: Mount Analogue
cards.description: René Daumal's unfinished posthumously published novel about a mountain-continent larger than any known mountain, remaining invisible due to curvature of space. The protagonist Father Pierre Sogol's name is a pataphysical reversal of Logos.

cards.title: Falsehood of Truth
cards.description: One of René Daumal's brilliant demonstrations isolating error as the principle of creation and existence, for one tiny truth would obliterate the entire universe by its wonder. This advanced sense of cosmic humor has no other name than Jarry's pataphysics.

cards.title: Satanic Devices
cards.description: In Mount Analogue, Pierre Sogol's inventions including a pen that spatters every five minutes, a portable phonograph crying 'Who do you think you are?', a pneumatic cushion that deflates unexpectedly, and a mirror reflecting faces as pig heads.

cards.title: Probable Systems
cards.description: bpNichol's instantiations of pataphysical cognition gathered in zygal (1985) and Art Facts (1990). Systems exploring lettristic patalogic, time machines, gematria, and linguistic transformations through playful manipulation of language.

cards.title: Lettristic Patalogic
cards.description: A logic devised to reveal hidden sentences in single words through application of rules to letter sequences. Example: if l is on g and l is in e, then the meaning of alphabetic sequence 'e f g' becomes clear through pataphysical reasoning.

cards.title: Brissetism
cards.description: Jean-Jacques Lecercle's term for linguistic insanity where phonetic similarities are treated as semantic identities. Named after Jean-Pierre Brisset, who believed wishing and fishing to be identical because 'wish' and 'fish' share sounds.

cards.title: Base Alphabet
cards.description: bpNichol's letter-number equation where A is assigned numerical value of 1 through Z with value of 26. A variant on Kabbalistic practice of assigning letters numerical values, opening possibility of poetical mathematics (faith=44=hope).

cards.title: Gematria
cards.description: Jewish mystical practice of assigning numerical values to letters, used by bpNichol in Probable Systems 4 (dedicated to 'James Joyce in his worst bummer'). Opens up possibility of poetical mathematics where words with same numerical value are equivalent.

cards.title: Sign and Design
cards.description: Alfred Kallir's 1961 book theorizing psychogenetic development of the alphabet from primordial signs in collective unconscious. Letters encode magic chain of procreative symbols (A=ox head/virility, B=breasts/belly, C=womb open for impregnation).

cards.title: 'Pataphysicians avec Apostrophe
cards.description: Artists who consciously identify with Jarry's pataphysics and explicitly reference it in their work, such as Robert Wyatt, Peter Blegvad, and Pere Ubu. Distinguished from unconscious pataphysicians who show influence without claiming it.

cards.title: Potache
cards.description: French word referring to adolescent schoolboy humor, particularly scatological. Jarry played this role with particular verve and conviction, loath to renounce it even in adulthood. Influenced pataphysical artists from Frank Zappa to Captain Beefheart.

cards.title: Oulipo
cards.description: Literary movement known for voluntary submission to linguistic constraints including palindromes, acrostics, lipograms, rhopalics, and homophonic translations. An offshoot of the Collège de 'Pataphysique.

cards.title: Plunderphonics
cards.description: Pioneered by composer John Oswald, the practice of making unofficial but recognizable musical quotes by plundering well-known pop and rock songs and drastically manipulating/rearranging them. A form of pataphysical clinamen in music.

cards.title: Xenochrony
cards.description: Frank Zappa's technique of extracting a musical part (like a guitar solo) and inserting it into a different song, sometimes combining parts from different time signatures and years. A pataphysical approach to musical composition treating time as non-linear.

cards.title: Science of Imaginary Solutions
cards.description: The primary definition of 'pataphysics from Faustroll: the science which symbolically attributes the properties of objects, described by their virtuality, to their lineaments. A science that examines the laws governing exceptions and explains the universe supplementary to this one.

cards.title: Jocoserious
cards.description: A term coined by Joyce describing art that combines humor and seriousness. Jarry's pataphysics embodies this quality decades before Joyce named it. Duchamp called for art with humor that added a note of seriousness. The deeply serious humor at the substance of pataphysics.

cards.title: Neo-Scientific Novel
cards.description: Jarry's designation for Exploits and Opinions of Dr. Faustroll, Pataphysician. A novel form that blurs the lines between fiction and nonfiction, literature and science, nonsense and philosophy. It situates science in the realm of the imaginary and speculative while making use of actual scientific and mathematical theories.

cards.title: Dr. Faustroll
cards.description: The protagonist of Jarry's Exploits and Opinions of Dr. Faustroll, Pataphysician. An ultrasexagenarian ephebe who travels from Paris to Paris by sieve. The Inamovable Curator of the Collège de 'Pataphysique. More than a character representing Jarry, Faustroll embodies an artistic-scientific energy, a continually unrolling force that creates and reveals alternative realities.

cards.title: Ubu Roi
cards.description: Jarry's 1896 play that caused a riot on opening night with its first word 'Merdre!' The play is a caricature featuring Père Ubu, a character with three mismatched teeth, a retractable ear, an immense belly, and an insatiable infantile ego. Cited as the birth of absurdist theatre and, for some, of French modernism and the avant-garde.

cards.title: Merdre
cards.description: The French word for 'shit' with an extra 'r', the first word of Ubu Roi that caused a riot. A thinly veiled expletive that symbolically threw excrement at the audience, eliciting both outraged protests and uproarious laughter. Ubu's signature robotic repetition reveals him as the embodiment of Bergsonian comedy—laughable as he reminds us of a mere machine.

cards.title: Epiphenomenalism
cards.description: The philosophical concept that consciousness itself might be an accidental side effect of the state of the brain. While Henri Bergson rejected this notion, Jarry was fascinated by it. A reference to epiphenomenalism precedes the two most famous definitions of 'pataphysics in Faustroll, suggesting the concept is crucial to understanding 'pataphysics.

cards.title: Equivalent Islands
cards.description: The fourteen islands Faustroll visits on his voyage from Paris to Paris, each corresponding to a different artistic figure including Aubrey Beardsley, Paul Valéry, Paul Gauguin, Henri Rousseau, and Stéphane Mallarmé. Jarry paints a composite portrait of avant-garde Paris and suggests pataphysical practice extends outside the world of the book into the real.

cards.title: Livres Pairs
cards.description: Equivalent books. Jarry often played with notions of equivalence and difference, asserting simultaneously that books could be equivalent and that, when examined closely, things that appear similar are not (A ≠ A).

cards.title: Ethernity
cards.description: A truly pataphysical realm completely outside of time and space. In book 7 of Faustroll, the protagonist sinks his sieve and drowns himself, making 'the gesture of dying' and is translated to ethernity. From ethernity, Faustroll offers a geometrical proof of God's existence leading to the solution '8 = 0'.

cards.title: Ultrasexagenarian Ephebe
cards.description: Dr. Faustroll's designation—someone who is both over sixty years old (ultrasexagenarian) and a young man or adolescent (ephebe). A pataphysical contradiction embodied. From Jarry's epigraph: 'Like a musical score, all art and all science were written in the curves of the limbs of the ultrasexagenarian ephebe.'

cards.title: Bosse-de-Nage
cards.description: Dr. Faustroll's cabin boy, a hydrocephalic dog-faced baboon capable of only one utterance: 'Ha ha.' Bosse-de-Nage personifies the instinctual reaction that, together with Panmuphle's intellectual seriousness, represents mutually exclusive opposites that Faustroll (and Jarry) insist can coexist. In pataphysical logic: 1 + 1 = 3.

cards.title: Panmuphle
cards.description: A bailiff sent to collect overdue rent from Faustroll who becomes the frame-tale narrator of the novel and an ironic connection to the rational, real world. Faustroll takes him prisoner, throws him into his wine-and-spirits-filled cellar, shackles him to the skiff, and forces him to row while 'drunk as I was and ready to believe anything.'

cards.title: Sieve (Vessel)
cards.description: Dr. Faustroll's vessel for his voyage from Paris to Paris: a twelve-meter-long copper-mesh skiff that floats on water by surface tension. The real challenge of setting sail in a sieve is keeping water from seeping in—it requires constant attention to bailing out the boat to maintain precarious balance at the intersection of imminent submersion and pataphysical buoyancy.

cards.title: Clinamen
cards.description: One of the Collège de 'Pataphysique's idiosyncratic concepts with uniquely pataphysical connotations. The smallest possible aberration or swerve. A term borrowed from Lucretius referring to the unpredictable swerve of atoms that allows for free will and spontaneous variation in an otherwise deterministic universe.

cards.title: Syzygy (Pataphysical)
cards.description: One of the Collège's idiosyncratic concepts. In astronomy, the alignment of three celestial bodies. In pataphysics, it represents moments of conjunction where opposites meet without canceling each other out. The Collège uses this term with uniquely pataphysical connotations.

cards.title: Antinomy
cards.description: One of the Collège's idiosyncratic concepts. A contradiction between two beliefs or conclusions that are in themselves reasonable. Pataphysics thrives on antinomy—it is the science that doesn't resolve contradictions but rather celebrates and systematically explores them.

cards.title: Disoccultation
cards.description: The Collège de 'Pataphysique's term for making something public or visible that was previously hidden or secret. The Collège was notoriously esoteric and insular, especially before its disoccultation in 2001, when it began to make its publications more widely available after decades of clandestine circulation.

cards.title: Collège de 'Pataphysics
cards.description: A parodically formal collective formed in Paris in 1948 to explore 'the science.' With Dr. Faustroll as Inamovable Curator, Marcel Duchamp as Grand Satrap, and members including Boris Vian, Max Ernst, Raymond Queneau, Man Ray, Jean Dubuffet, Groucho Marx, and Eugène Ionesco. An intentional contradiction in terms: a 'pataphysical institution.'

cards.title: Oulipo
cards.description: Ouvroir de Littérature Potentielle—Workshop of Potential Literature. A subcommittee of the Collège de 'Pataphysique founded in 1960 by François Le Lionnais and Raymond Queneau. Consists of mathematicians and writers researching and creating forms and constraints with which literature has been or could be written.

cards.title: Anoulipism
cards.description: The Oulipo's term for research into forms and constraints that have already been used in literature. The analytical side of Oulipian practice, studying what has been done. Complements synthoulipism, which creates new forms.

cards.title: Synthoulipism
cards.description: The Oulipo's term for the creation of new forms and constraints for literature. The creative side of Oulipian practice, inventing what could be. Complements anoulipism, which studies existing forms.

cards.title: Recurrent Literature
cards.description: Any text that contains, explicitly or implicitly, generative rules that invite the reader to pursue the production of the text to infinity or until exhaustion of interest or attention. The Oulipo's investment in systematic, procedural, and exhaustive experimentation grounded in jocoserious constraints.

cards.title: Black Humor
cards.description: A form of humor that finds comedy in disturbing, morbid, or taboo subjects. Jarry was included in André Breton's Anthology of Black Humor (1939) and was influential on many avant-garde artists who employed this aesthetic. Apollinaire declared that thanks to Jarry, humor arose from the lower regions and now furnishes the poet with entirely original lyricism.

cards.title: Theatre of the Absurd
cards.description: A theatrical movement popular in the United States during the 1950s and 1960s, deeply influenced by Jarry's work. Includes works by Samuel Beckett, Eugène Ionesco, and Edward Albee. Ubu Roi is cited as the birth of absurdist theatre.

cards.title: Pataphysical Calendar
cards.description: An idiosyncratic dating system used by the Collège de 'Pataphysique as part of its parodic formality. One of several esoteric practices that cultivated the Collège's reputation as a secret society producing secret knowledge.

cards.title: Canadian "Pataphysics
cards.description: The term coined by the Toronto Research Group (TRG), the collaboration between poets bpNichol and Steve McCaffery (1973-1981). Adds an apostrophe to 'pataphysics, denoting 'a shift from elision to quotation.' If 'Pataphysics is the science of imaginary solutions, then "Pataphysics (via double elision) is 'the literature of all imaginary sciences.'

cards.title: Definitional Literature
cards.description: A technique pioneered by Stefan Themerson and developed by the Oulipo, with experiments by Georges Perec and Marcel Bénabou. Literature that uses definitions as its primary compositional method, interrogating the metaphors of 'machine' and 'engine' as they relate to textual production.

cards.title: Twisted Witz
cards.description: Experiments in psychopathology and humor by Dr. Faustroll and his pataphysical progeny. Freud discovered 'the wit (Witz) of all unconscious processes,' asserting that joke-work and dream-work must be identical in some essential respect. Jarry twisted Witz inside out, investigating joke-work and dream-work artistically before they existed theoretically.

cards.title: Gidouille
cards.description: Père Ubu's immense belly, so big and singular in appetite that it required the invention of its own term. Represents Ubu's insatiable, infantile ego and embodiment of pure appetite and defecation. Part of Ubu's appearance along with three mismatched teeth and a retractable ear.

cards.title: Gay Science / La Gaya Scienza
cards.description: Nietzsche's concept (la gaya scienza / fröhliche Wissenschaft) meaning joyful wisdom or the cheerful science. The tempo indication for the overture of Ubu Roi was 'gaîment.' Jarry's entire oeuvre is pervaded by a cheerful tempo of rational madness devoted to creating his own 'fröhliche Wissenschaft.'

cards.title: Ship of Fools / Narrenschiff
cards.description: A motif particularly prominent in Rabelais, reflecting the Medieval-Renaissance practice of putting the mentally ill on ships with the triple function of curing, confining, and exhibiting them. Jarry employs this motif but inverts it: Dr. Faustroll, the mad scientist-captain, takes Panmuphle, representative of law and rationality, prisoner.

cards.title: Psychopathology
cards.description: The study of mental disorders. Dr. Faustroll can be seen as a case study: a suicidal, homicidal, alcoholic, homeless man who claims to telepathically communicate with dead scientists and displays textbook megalomania. Jarry could fit in several DSM-5 boxes: schizophrenia, OCD, alcoholism, bipolar disorder, suicidality, and Tourette's syndrome.

cards.title: Hermeneutic Paranoia
cards.description: A compulsion induced by Faustroll to constantly search for hidden signs, symbols, and allusions. The text leads readers to see signs everywhere, interpret every aspect, suspect meaning under each particle of the work. One is immersed in an ocean of endless meaning and interconnection from the theological to the mathematical.

cards.title: Anti-Hermeneutic Paranoia
cards.description: The equal but opposite paranoia to hermeneutic paranoia. The reader must always be alert to the interpretive bomb of the joke, pun, or ironic tone that reverses stable meaning and points to the possibility that all apparent interconnection is just an illusion, prank, or blague. Readers can be driven crazy by the possibility of lack of meaning.

cards.title: Stream of Consciousness
cards.description: William James's concept that Jarry likely encountered through Henri Bergson. Faustroll is arguably the first full-fledged modernist stream-of-consciousness novel, and much of its humor stems from its revelation of the incongruous thoughts, images, words, sensations, and desires constantly flowing through the mind.

cards.title: Joke-Work
cards.description: Freud's term for the mechanisms by which jokes are constructed, which he asserted must be identical to dream-work in some essential respect. This equivalency is based on his discovery of 'the wit (Witz) of all unconscious processes.' Jarry investigated joke-work artistically before it existed theoretically and scientifically.

cards.title: Dream-Work
cards.description: Freud's term for the mechanisms by which dreams are constructed. Freud asserted that dream-work and joke-work must be identical in some essential respect, an equivalency based on the wit (Witz) of unconscious processes. Jarry investigated dream-work artistically before it existed theoretically.

cards.title: Phynance
cards.description: The Collège de 'Pataphysique's idiosyncratic spelling of 'finance.' Part of the Collège's use of esoteric spellings, dates, and concepts that cultivated its reputation as a secret society producing secret knowledge. One of many neologisms and alternative spellings that add to pataphysical mystique.

cards.title: Precocious Imbecility
cards.description: The diagnosis Jarry received that released him from military service. He probably took pride in this diagnosis and would have scoured the DSM-5 attempting to cultivate even more scientifically recognized symptoms of insanity. Much of his existence was devoted to an extended psychological experiment on himself.

cards.title: Surface Tension
cards.description: The scientific principle by which Dr. Faustroll's copper-mesh sieve floats on water. An actual physical phenomenon applied to an absurd situation. Exemplifies how Faustroll uses real scientific concepts in imaginary solutions, making us question where the boundary between real science and pataphysical science lies.

cards.title: Témoins Oculistes
cards.description: Oculist witnesses. Part of the bachelor machine in the lower portion of The Large Glass, along with the chocolate grinder and the cemetery for uniforms and liveries (the nine malic molds). Duchamp's term later adopted into pataphysical vocabulary. Represents the realm of the bachelors desiring the bride above without possibility of mutual comprehension.

cards.title: Polysemy / Polyphony
cards.description: The capacity for a sign or word to have multiple meanings (polysemy) or multiple voices (polyphony). The realization that there is not a single source of truth but multiple perspective-dependent truths began to coalesce with Jarry and expanded massively in following decades. Jarry put polysemic play into play, bringing it into influential avant-garde circles.

cards.title: Pataphallics
cards.description: Jean-Michel Rabaté's term for analyzing the psychosexual aspects of Jarry's novels Messaline and Le Surmâle. While later instantiations of pataphysics tend not to be explicitly sexual, this reading asks us to reconsider the relationship between pataphysics and sexuality and Jarry's significant impact on French modernism.

cards.title: Wartime Pataphysics
cards.description: Catherine Hansen's term for pataphysical activities and sympathies in the years surrounding WWII and leading to the Collège's founding in 1948. Includes the Main à Plume group, Noël Arnaud, Boris Rybak, and Rybak's Intraphysics. Situated in wider sociocultural contexts of myth-making and myth-busting at the onset of the Cold War.

cards.title: Intraphysics
cards.description: Boris Rybak's concept developed during the wartime period. Part of the pataphysical activities that occurred in the years leading up to the founding of the Collège de 'Pataphysique in 1948. Associated with the Main à Plume group and postwar surrealism.

cards.title: Main à Plume
cards.description: A group active during wartime with pataphysical sympathies. Part of the activities leading up to the founding of the Collège de 'Pataphysique in 1948. Associated with Noël Arnaud, Boris Rybak, and connections to André Breton's surrealists during and after WWII.

cards.title: Grand Satrap
cards.description: A title in the parodically formal hierarchy of the Collège de 'Pataphysique. Marcel Duchamp held this title. Part of the Collège's elaborate system of ranks and titles that simultaneously mock and honor institutional structures.

cards.title: Inamovable Curator
cards.description: Dr. Faustroll's title in the Collège de 'Pataphysique. As the protagonist of Jarry's defining work, Faustroll serves as the permanent, immovable spiritual leader of the institution. The title suggests authority that cannot be removed or transferred.

cards.title: Apostrophe in 'Pataphysics
cards.description: Though Jarry rarely used it, he wrote that the actual orthography of 'pataphysics should include the apostrophe 'so as to avoid a simple pun.' The Collège's 1965 recommendation: only 'Pataphysics (the Science) gets the apostrophe substantively; the adjective does not. The apostrophe is beloved for its eccentric flair and inherent protest to alphabetized dictionaries.

cards.title: Organ-Planet Correspondence
cards.description: Rudolf Steiner's anthroposophical teaching on the microcosm-macrocosm relationship between human organs and celestial bodies. 'For this reason they called the spleen "saturnine," or an inner Saturn in man; and, similarly, the liver they called an inner Jupiter; and the gallbladder, an inner Mars.' This hermetic tradition of correspondences—where the human body contains planetary forces—resonates with Elden Ring's cosmic anatomy, where the Elden Ring itself functions as the governing principle binding celestial order to bodily form.
